data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Thousands of rail workers to lose jobs under Labour’s nationalisation plans
data.description:
Thousands of rail workers are to be made redundant as part of Labour’s plan to nationalise the railways.

Network Rail is making cuts to its workforce as it faces a budget shortfall potentially as high as £1.8bn, insiders said.

An estimated 2,000 employees out of the organisation’s total headcount of 41,000 could be laid off in the coming months.

Network Rail bosses told staff the proposed job cuts were needed as part of preparations for the track maintenance company to be absorbed into Great British Railways (GBR).

Earlier, Rachel Reeves revealed Labour could spend up to £45bn of taxpayers’ money on the Northern Powerhouse Rail project, including a possible extension of the controversial HS2 railway line to Manchester.

An all-staff message issued by Clive Berrington, Network Rail’s group director of route services, suggested the cuts were necessary to cope with “increased demands” and in preparation for the company’s merger with GBR.

He said: “We are facing increased demands that require additional resources, particularly in areas such as cyber security, new telecoms services, and lifts and escalators.

“We are also evaluating what needs to be done to get Route Services ready for GBR.”

Dave Hooper, Network Rail’s managing director for its southern region, said in a separate bulletin that there was a £350m gap in funding for his area alone between now and the year 2029.

The bulletin, seen by The Telegraph, said: “We’ve explained the demands on our funding and that there’s no additional money available.

“In fact, our [funding] settlement assumes we deliver significant efficiencies, which increase every year of the Control Period.”

Cuts to affect junior managerial grades

GBR is at the heart of Labour’s flagship plan to take passenger trains into full state ownership and control, ending rail privatisation reforms introduced by John Major in the mid-1990s.

The redundancies will affect staff in Bands 1 to 4, which are understood to be junior managerial grades. Affected staff will be formally placed at risk of redundancy from next Tuesday, The Telegraph understands.

Network Rail’s taxpayer funding is allocated in five-year control periods. The current period, known as CP7, ends in 2029, which is the latest year in which a general election could be held.

Speaking to the BBC on Tuesday morning, Ms Reeves claimed Labour’s promised increase in public spending on the railways in the 2030s is “actually going to happen”.

She said: “We’re doing this properly, doing this right. Because I want to give confidence to people that this is actually going to happen.

“It’s going to improve your train links, whether you live in Pudsey or Rotherham, or Newcastle or Darlington, or in the centres of Manchester and Liverpool, and Bradford and Sheffield.

“You’re going to see benefits from this.”

Although Labour has said the total budget for Northern Powerhouse Rail is capped at £45bn, just £1.1bn of taxpayers’ money has been allocated to design and feasibility studies so far.

Michael Dnes, a transport consultant, suggested this week that Labour’s announcement about using the former HS2 route between Manchester and Birmingham for a new, standard-speed railway line had only come about because legal powers authorising land purchase and track construction are due to expire soon.

“In case you’re unclear, yes, this is HS2 coming back – at least in the sense that it’s the HS2 rail line,” he said, adding that the line is “not being built at any measurable point in the future”.

A rail industry source who requested to be anonymous said: “It’s all linked to the creation of GBR and resizing the organisation for that.”

Regarding the redundancies at Network Rail, a spokesperson said: “We’re committed to delivering a safe, reliable railway while reducing costs for taxpayers and meeting our £3.9bn efficiency target for this control period.

“To achieve this, we’ve reviewed how we operate across the business, including management structures, to ensure we’re as efficient as possible.

“Our review is now concluding, and we’ll be sharing proposals with colleagues and trade unions for discussion over the coming weeks before any final decisions are made.”

The RMT trade union confirmed it was aware of the redundancy plan but said it would not affect the trade union’s members, who are frontline workers rather than managerial staff.

Network Rail later denied claims that its redundancies were linked to Labour’s rail nationalisation plans. 

A spokesman said: “GBR has not yet been created. The work ongoing at Network Rail is about delivering our efficiency commitments for CP7 – it is not part of the planning for GBR, nor linked to nationalisation or Government decisions.”

The Transport Salaried Staffs’ Association, which represents affected managers, did not respond to a request for comment.

The DfT declined to comment.

data.title: ‘I have 48 tins of tomatoes’: The middle-class obsession with bulk buying
data.description:
Stepping into Costco for the very first time, one could be forgiven for thinking the end of the world is nigh. On my maiden outing last month, other shoppers looked like they were stocking their doomsday bunkers, heaving trolleys loaded with pallets of mineral water, slabs of meat the size of a small child and enough toilet roll to supply an army regiment.

In truth, I arrived at Costco’s vast Southampton outpost already sceptical. Bulk buying, I had always thought, belonged to a species of consumer that wasn’t me: hyper-organised, storage-rich and willing to put inordinate effort into saving just a couple of pounds. I had also assumed a narrow range of joyless products – jeroboams of cheap ketchup, sack-loads of indifferent flour – and because I don’t have the space to stockpile my groceries, I was fully prepared to buy nothing at all. I just wanted to see what all the fuss was about.

At first, the cavernous warehouse space and industrial shelving stacked with gigantic tins, jars and sacks, arranged in no apparent logical order, did nothing to convert me. It felt less like grocery shopping – something I generally enjoy – and more like navigating a storage facility that happened to sell food. But Costco isn’t a global behemoth for nothing, and my epiphany duly came in aisle 12. An immense jar of dried wild mushrooms – the kind of specialist goody I didn’t expect to find – was extraordinary value: at £10.99 for 340g, almost 70 per cent cheaper, gram for gram, than the stingy packets I regularly buy at Waitrose.

Once I had my eye in, it was all systems go. A chunky 1.4kg container of chia seeds (I eat them for breakfast daily, woe is me) for £7.99 was, again, roughly two-thirds cheaper than at Waitrose. Then there was a hunk of 30-month-aged parmesan, a little over £20 for 1.48kg (more than 50 per cent cheaper), and a litre of Iliada extra virgin olive oil – a brand favoured by chefs – for £7.99 (£11-13 elsewhere). With my two adult children and husband toing and froing to taste samples and forage, our trolley filled with unseemly speed. But really, I shouldn’t have been surprised.

Costco UK is an outpost of a vast American membership warehouse club founded in Seattle in 1983, with more than 900 warehouses globally and 145 million members. As I learnt, it sells almost everything one would expect from a mainstream supermarket, but in bigger quantities: fresh fruit and vegetables, meat, fish, cheese and baked goods, alongside a vast range of non-perishable food and household items. The curveball aisles are an added draw: a widescreen television, contact lenses and a leaf blower can be yours in a single sweep.

UK branches are thriving

In the US, Costco is an institution, ranked among the largest companies in the Fortune 500, its annual membership model binding shoppers in for the long haul. Strict membership criteria notwithstanding – only business owners and certain professions qualify, with fees ranging from £30 to £84 depending on the plan – the UK arm is thriving. Last year it reported pre-tax profits of £188.9m, a 35 per cent rise on the previous year. Twenty-nine Costco warehouses are now scattered across England, Scotland and Wales, with at least 14 more reportedly on the horizon, including one under way in Gloucester.

That financial success and growth speaks to a shift in the way many middle-class Britons are shopping. My fellow trolley-pushers weren’t stockpiling for Armageddon; they were chasing value and convenience. It’s a trend that took hold during the pandemic, when shortages – most memorably of toilet roll – pushed people into shopping big for key items, says Nick Carroll, the director of retail insights at consumer group Mintel. And it didn’t stop when restrictions were lifted. A Barclaycard survey in 2022 found that 35 per cent of Britons admitted to stockpiling everyday items such as pasta, tinned food and toilet roll, as rising prices replaced lockdown as the dominant concern. “Consumers have held on to those more savvy shopping behaviours,” Carroll says. “Bulk buying is certainly more popular than it was five or six years ago.”

Perhaps surprisingly, the well-off are leading the charge. “It’s more common among middle-income and affluent households,” Carroll explains. “If you’re on a lower income, you’re unlikely to buy six bottles of something you won’t need for many months, because that money is better spent elsewhere. Typically, it’s people who are being electively savvy, or simply cautious, and who have the money to spend a little more now in order to make savings further down the line.”

A walk-in pantry helps

Mintel research underlines this pattern. In 2023, 17 per cent of British consumers shopped with Costco, rising to 30 per cent among households with incomes above £75,000 – the same group most likely to have the space, whether a larger home, spacious garage or walk-in pantry, to store the bounty from these mega-shops.

Costco isn’t the only way of buying big. Many do it closer to home, through a proliferation of multibuy offers or larger pack sizes at mainstream supermarkets. Online shopping has made this easier still. Mintel reports that more than a quarter of Britons who shop online do so specifically to bulk buy, as home delivery avoids the back-breaking problem of transporting heavy loads home.

Beyond the supermarkets, a small but established group of UK-based online wholesalers – including Bulk Buy Direct, Low Price Foods and Approved Food – is selling large formats of discounted food, often surplus or sometimes near or past their best-before dates. Food subscriptions are also on the rise, with small, quality brands in particular offering significant discounts for multiple items delivered on a regular schedule.

Bold Beans, a specialist in premium beans sold in jars, says subscription revenues in 2025 rose by 60 per cent on the year before. “Customers tell us that subscribing makes meal planning easier,” a spokesman says, adding that fans of the product simply don’t want to run out. On the subject of running out, one friend, who prefers the Mutti brand of tinned tomatoes, on hearing a rumour that a poor harvest could cause shortages in coming months, recently hurried to “the big Tesco” to buy 48 cans. These now occupy most of her larder space.

Coffee subscriptions are percolating too, driven by soaring and volatile bean prices. Rave Coffee, an independent Gloucestershire roastery, says it saw a 56 per cent increase in subscriptions last year compared with 2024. “Customers want the certainty they won’t run out of coffee and won’t blow the budget,” says David Webb, its marketing director.

Kev Lewis, the founder of Coffee Blog, says coffee lovers are increasingly looking for predictability, both in price and quality. “Subscriptions allow customers effectively to lock in a price, which feels reassuring in an uncertain market, almost like buying a small future in a commodity.”

An evolutionary drive to save

But bulk buying delivers benefits beyond the financial, say enthusiasts: it’s simply reassuring to know that favourite products won’t run out. This taps into something essentially human, says consumer psychologist Philip Graves. “It’s almost an instinctive response to want to stock up, which connects to a primary evolutionary drive to save,” he says. “Historically, humans did better once they realised there would be times when resources were scarce, and that storing helped protect them.” It’s also appealing to reduce the number of shopping trips for dull basics, leaving more time for more enjoyable things, Graves adds.

For all these reasons, podcaster Olly Mann, 44, from Hertfordshire, is a Costco evangelist. He’s been shopping there since childhood – his father was an early convert – and his nearest branch is a convenient 20-minute drive away. “I buy in big quantities for the convenience of not having to think about certain things again and again,” he says. “For really boring items like dishwasher tabs and loo roll, you buy big packets and you literally don’t have to think about them again for months.”

Mann doesn’t price-compare before he shops, and acknowledges that some items, such as meat, aren’t necessarily cheaper than at his local supermarket. But he knows his way to the bargains. Kirkland Signature is Costco’s private-label brand, which Mann says is high quality. “Their filter coffee [1.4kg for £18.99] lasts me months of daily home brewing,” he says. Now and then he treats himself to a vast bottle of Kirkland French vodka (1.75 litres for £40.99). “It keeps me in martinis for a year,” he says. Wrapping paper, cards and boxes of chocolates are also regular Christmas purchases. “The quality is what you might expect at Harrods, but for a fraction of the price.”

Catherine Balavage Yardley, a freelance writer from Wandsworth, bulk buys everything from toothpaste to children’s clothes. A working mother of four, including a newborn, and without a car, she shops big online from Amazon, Ocado, Boots, M&S, Tesco and Sainsbury’s. Saving money, she says, isn’t the main driver.

She is partly motivated by a fear of running out of essentials. “During Covid, our garage was probably half full of water and other stuff,” she says. But stocking up is mainly about making life easier. “It saves me so much time, which is more valuable to me than anything else.” She rarely buys just one of anything, opting instead for multiple or oversized packs of soap, toothpaste, toilet roll, children’s clothes and shoes, toys (for birthday presents), fruit, bottled water and milk. And bourbon biscuits. “My kids go through tons and tons of those,” she says.

Convenience, though, has its costs. Although her home is large, storage is still an issue when shopping on this scale; cupboards are crammed and the garage absorbs the overspill. “If you opened it up you would just see chaos and a million Amazon boxes,” she says. As a result, she sometimes forgets what she has bought and ends up buying duplicates. “Sometimes you can overbuy,” she admits. On one occasion, children’s shoes she had squirrelled away surfaced too late. “By the time I remembered they were there, he’d grown out of them,” she says.

Al Baker, a money-saving expert and author of the Penny Pincher blog, says storage is only one of the challenges when buying in bulk. A devotee of shopping big – “much to my wife’s displeasure” – he argues that it only really works if you are disciplined. Without that, he says, it’s easy to overspend on things you don’t need or simply don’t have room for.

He also points out that bulk buying is only really an option if you have the money to spare. “If you’ve got a few quid sat in the bank and you think you can afford to do it, brilliant. If you haven’t, that’s an issue.”

Baker’s home sounds like a small warehouse as he explains where he stores his monthly big shop. A chest freezer takes pride of place in the living room, filled with portioned meals he preps and freezes every Sunday, while he has equipped a cupboard under the stairs with shelves to hold toilet roll and kitchen paper. That cupboard now acts as a brake on his shopping habits: if there’s no room left, he won’t restock, no matter how good the bargain – though he admits the rule doesn’t always hold, and the back of his van is currently filled with overspill.

More important than storage, Baker says, is doing the sums properly before a bulk shop, rather than assuming a bumper pack is automatically good value. That means checking unit prices, comparing like with like across retailers, and factoring in other deals. “Supermarkets can be surprisingly competitive once you take account of multibuy offers, loyalty card discounts and cashback,” he says. “You have to factor all of that in.” He recommends tools such as Trolley, a free UK grocery price-comparison site and app that lets shoppers compare prices across major supermarkets and see where a basket comes out cheapest.

Another hazard is over-indulging. You might steadily plough through a vast supply of dishwasher tabs, but put a case of chocolate bars within easy reach and it may disappear far faster than it ever would in smaller quantities.

‘Meat can be more expensive’

Sophie Bowd, from Swindon, is well aware of these pitfalls. She and her partner spend £900-1,000 a month on a main Costco shop for groceries and household items for themselves and their four children, topping up elsewhere during the week. “We used to shop at Asda or Tesco, but we were spending about £1,500 a month,” she says. “Now we do one shop at Costco, then a weekly top-up of about £50.” That amounts to a saving of around £300-400 a month.

Bowd, who works in finance and runs a TikTok account charting her shops, knows that buying in bulk doesn’t necessarily mean securing a bargain. “The meat can be more expensive, especially beef, but it’s always better quality, so I don’t mind paying more for that,” she says. Snacks for children’s lunch boxes deliver some of the biggest savings, as does milk. “You can get two big cartons for under £3, almost double what you get in a normal shop for the same price.”

But shopping like this involves a full day’s work. Bowd’s nearest Costco, in Reading, is a 45-minute drive and, with four children – one still in a pram – the family can only manage one trolley at a time. It’s a slog, she admits, but one that pays off. “I’ve always got food in, so we never fall into the trap of getting takeaways,” she says. “And I’m saving money.”

If the idea of bulk-buying your entire shop gives you conniptions, a selective approach might make more sense, particularly for items where prices are expected to keep rising this year. Coffee is a good place to start, with global supplies under pressure. Lock into a subscription or buy big bags and freeze small batches in airtight containers or freezer bags.

Olive oil is another candidate, with prices expected to remain high and unpredictable. Buy large tins or bottles – the oil you use every day is better for this than the special stuff – store somewhere cool and dark, and decant into smaller containers as you go. Chocolate, too, looks unlikely to get cheaper. Dark chocolate can keep for up to two years if stored somewhere cool, dry and dark in an airtight container. Milk chocolate should store well for a year. Beef prices could also rise this year, so if you eat it often and have the freezer space, buying in bulk and freezing portions could deliver savings.

Bulk buying only really works when it’s based on what you already consume, argues Graves. “Think back to what you’ve used consistently over the last few months,” he says. “Our past behaviour is generally our best guide to what you’ll use in the future.” In other words, the safest bulk buys are often the boring ones.

He’s not wrong. I’m still staring at a vast (1.5kg) jar of pretzels I bought at Costco – something I never usually buy but convinced myself I’d get through over Christmas. It’s still two-thirds full.

data.title: Treasury doesn’t understand risks of shadow banking bubble, Lords warn
data.description:
Treasury ministers have come under fire from an influential group of peers for failing to grasp the risks of Wall Street’s shadow banking bubble.

On Thursday, the House of Lords’ financial services regulation committee heavily criticised the Government for having “demonstrated a limited grasp” of the risks posed to the financial system from the private markets boom.

The report said the Treasury had an attitude of “passivity”, which was all the more surprising given the potential risks to the UK’s financial stability from shadow banking and private markets.

The rapid growth of private credit, which is corporate loans made by non-bank lenders, has raised concerns about its stability and the potential impact of a crash on the wider financial system.

Concerns about weakening lending standards were fuelled last year by the collapse of two US companies – First Brands, a car parts supplier, and Tricolor, a subprime auto lender.

Jamie Dimon, the head of JPMorgan, warned that more “cockroaches” would emerge because of lax lending standards over the years.

Lord Hollick, a member of the committee and former boss of media giant UBM, said: “The Treasury itself needs to be curious about this and needs to be actively engaged in looking for this and finding this information and giving us their view because obviously a lot of savers’ money, pension funds and insurance companies are going to be tied up with this.

“Heaven forbid that anything should go wrong, but clearly it’s of sufficient size and importance for the Treasury also to take a careful look at it.”

While the Lords committee praised the work of the Bank of England, led by Andrew Bailey, it warned that the Treasury appeared to defer to the Bank, with limited engagement with the issue itself.

The report concluded that there was not enough information to conclude whether private markets were “systemic” – in other words, whether they could pose a risk to the wider financial system.

In particular, it praised the Bank for its plan to conduct the world’s first “stress test” of the shadow banking sector to see how it would cope with a global shock.

The exercise will run over two rounds, with results expected to be published in early 2027.

While tougher regulations for banks following the financial crisis have boosted their resilience, they have also pushed some of their traditional lending activities outside the more regulated banking sector.

Britain’s private market has grown by 56pc since 2015 to $185bn (£138bn) and is the second-largest after the United States, the report said.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We have worked together with the regulators to significantly increase our focus of non-banks sectors in recent years and have a robust, flexible framework to protect financial stability.

“We are considering the committee’s recommendations and will respond in due course.”

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Amol Rajan’s sudden departure could be a blessing in disguise for Today
data.description:
Amol Rajan announced his departure from the Today programme in peak Amol Rajan style. He delivered his message via Instagram, standing in the street in his Del Boy sheepskin jacket, T-shirt and weighty neck chain.

He began by joking: “OK, so the rumours are true. I am indeed the next director-general of the BBC. As it happens, I’m also batting at number four for England and I’m running to be mayor of London.”

In fact, he is leaving to launch his own media empire in “the great digital Narnia of the creator economy”. At first glance, this looks like bad news for the Today programme. Rajan’s exit will strip BBC Radio 4’s flagship news and current affairs show of its biggest personality.

Yet it could also be a blessing in disguise, because Today is in decline and BBC bosses need to rethink what it should be.

While Rajan brought great energy when he joined in 2021, he also changed the tone of the programme with his matey, relaxed style. Today changed to accommodate him, not the other way around.

Rajan was followed in 2024 by Emma Barnett, whose easy familiarity with listeners (“Let’s start the week, shall we?”) is a reminder of her previous roles on Woman’s Hour and 5 Live and a far cry from the days of John Humphrys.

None of this is accidental. BBC bosses wanted a more youthful lineup. It hoped that the popularity of Rajan and Barnett in other spheres, from podcasts to Instagram, would bring in a new audience.

Instead, ratings have dwindled. The most recent figures were an audience of 5.4 million weekly listeners, down from nearly eight million in 2018.

Listeners who believe the BBC has failed on impartiality are departing for other outlets such as GB News. Meanwhile the more Left-leaning are flocking to podcasts such as The Rest Is Politics or The News Agents.

In the days of Humphrys, Jim Naughtie or Brian Redhead, politicians knew that an interview on the Today programme would make headlines – often to their detriment, if Humphrys gave them a savaging.

Now those politicians can speak directly to the public through social media and without challenge, so why bother with a radio programme?

Today has also lost its mojo, with a rotating cast of presenters and no central team. The departure of Mishal Husain a year ago (she was replaced by Anna Foster) left only Nick Robinson and Justin Webb representing the old guard.

Meanwhile, relations between Robinson and Barnett are said to be so strained that they don’t appear on the same show if they can help it.

Some Today insiders argue that the show should refocus on being a serious-minded news programme, cutting the showbiz content and focusing more on politics.

If he could be persuaded to change jobs and replace Rajan then “Chris Mason would be the prize”, says one, referring to the BBC’s political editor.

As for Rajan, his departure coincides with that of Tim Davie, who stepped down after the Telegraph published an investigation into BBC bias.

Davie was Rajan’s biggest champion and the two were great friends. The outgoing director-general will be on hand to offer advice when Rajan launches his new media business, an “empire of ideas and IP” that will encompass YouTube, podcasts, books, brand deals, live events and Substack.

“It’s time to unleash my inner Del Boy,” Rajan said.

Rajan has been candid about his mental health. He has described a panic attack in the hours before his first Today shift and confided that his father’s death in 2022 plunged him into a period of “profound turmoil about the point of it all”.

The father-of-four has also made no secret of the toll that Today’s early starts took on him, speaking of his struggles with sleep deprivation.

Barnett is also said to find the wake-up calls brutal, speaking recently about setting her alarm for 3:21am, prompting some to wonder how long she will stick it out.

Rajan has not severed ties with the BBC and will continue to present University Challenge, along with his Radical podcast. His was always a portfolio career, and the Today programme just one step on his journey.

data.title: Britain at risk of electricity rationing before general election
data.description:
Britain risks electricity rationing by the next general election unless its fleet of ageing gas-fired power stations is urgently upgraded, a new report has warned.

More than a dozen of Britain’s gas-fired power stations are so old that they may not last until the end of the decade and key parts required to upgrade them take years to arrive on order, energy consultancy Watt-Logic said.

It called on Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, to face up to the threat amid growing concern over UK power supplies, which still rely largely on gas despite the expansion of renewables.

“Without urgent action to secure dispatchable generation and stabilise the gas network, the UK faces escalating risks of supply shortfalls and widespread system failures well before 2030,” the report warns.

“It will be difficult to meet existing demand without rationing, let alone any additional demand from electrification.”

About a third of the UK’s electricity is generated by gas-fired power plants – but on windless days, this proportion can double.

Kathryn Porter, from Watt-Logic, warned that in five years time – just after the next general election – many of those gas plants could be offline and there are currently no plans to replace them.

She said a third of the fleet had been built in the 1990s and “while most received upgrades in the 2000s, in most cases this did not involve replacing rotors [a key component prone to wear]”.

“This means the anticipated retirement dates are becoming imminent and there is no replacement and minimal upgrade plans in place,” she added.

It means the risk of electricity rationing during the winter months will increase over the next five years – peaking between 2028 and 2031 – because of the loss in energy production.

The loss will be made worse by the planned shutdown of the UK’s ageing nuclear stations. Eight of the 10 remaining reactors face closure before or in 2030.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said: “This report lays out in expert detail how Britain’s net zero plans are taking us down a path of economic ruin. If ministers were truly interested in making Britain rich again, they would ditch their green ideology and take her report on board.”

Ms Porter warned that Scotland faces a particular peril because it has just two power stations capable of keeping power flowing through its grid – and both are at risk of failure.

Other gas-fired plants at risk of failure include Medway, Saltend and Seabank, all operated by energy giant SSE.

Connah’s Quay and Cottam, run by Uniper, and Little Barford, run by RWE, are also under threat.

Some new power stations are in planning or construction, including some gas-fired plants – but not enough to replace likely losses.

The report follows similar warnings from organisations including the National Energy System Operator (Neso), which last November warned that Britain faces the risk of severe gas shortages.

Responding to the report, a Neso spokesman said: “Great Britain has one of the most secure energy systems in the world... our engineers are confident Britain’s grid will continue to operate safely and securely as more renewables connect to the network in the years ahead.”

A spokesperson for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said: “The National Energy System Operator,  has been clear the faster we decarbonise, the more secure we are. 

“Gas will continue to play a key role in our energy system as we transition to clean, more secure, homegrown energy. 

“That’s why we are also delivering the biggest upgrade to Great Britain’s electricity network in decades to deliver clean power by 2030 and beyond.”

Uniper said it was confident its power stations would keep operating.

SSE, RWE and the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero were approached for comment.

data.title: Miliband prepared to add £1.8bn to energy bills with wind farm blitz
data.description:
Hundreds of new wind turbines are to be built around Britain’s coastline after Ed Miliband awarded yearly subsidies of up to £1.8bn to green energy developers.

The Energy Secretary has approved plans for six new offshore wind farms following an auction of renewables contracts known as Allocation Round Seven.

As one of the UK’s largest-ever subsidy rounds, the costs will be borne by billpayers over a period of 20 years under Labour’s plans to hit net zero.

Subsidies will gradually increase as the windfarms are constructed, potentially spiralling to £1.8bn a year by 2032-33.

The Energy Department argues that the extra wind generation will reduce the wholesale cost of power, making the actual impact on bills far lower.

The approvals will see three of the world’s largest wind farms built in the North Sea including at Dogger Bank off Yorkshire, Berwick Bank off the Firth of Forth and Norfolk Vanguard off East Anglia.

A fourth will be built off the coast of north Wales. Two small floating wind farms have also been approved, one off Pembrokeshire and the other off Scotland’s north coast.

The approved turbines are expected to go into operation from 2028, strengthening Mr Miliband’s efforts to achieve almost entirely clean power by the end of this Parliament.

Mr Miliband hailed the latest auction as a success, claiming it will deliver a record 8.4 gigawatts of offshore wind power – which is enough to power the equivalent of 12 million homes.

It is also expected to deliver £22bn of private investment from wind farm developers.

However, the extra burden on billpayers will increase scrutiny of the Energy Secretary’s net zero agenda as households are already having to pay for a raft of green levies and network costs.

Mr Miliband said: “With these results, Britain is taking back control of our energy sovereignty.

“This is a historic win for those who want Britain to stand on our own two feet, controlling our own energy rather than depending on markets controlled by petrostates and dictators.

“It is a monumental step towards clean power by 2030 and the price secured in this auction is 40pc lower than the alternative cost of building and operating a new gas plant.”

Critics dispute claims that new wind farms are cheaper than building gas plants. The £91 per megawatt hour (MWh) given to wind generators exceeds the average £80 per MWh for gas.

The latter price would be lower - around £55 - were it not for two carbon taxes, the Emissions Trading Scheme and the Carbon Price Support Scheme which together add about £25 to the price of electricity generated by burning gas.

Some of the individual schemes approved by Mr Miliband faced powerful opposition.

Berwick Bank, now the world’s largest planned offshore wind project, is opposed by conservation groups who say it will kill thousands of seabirds a year.

The latest auction means the developers will be awarded “contracts for difference”, guaranteeing them a minimum price averaging £91 per megawatt hour to ensure they don’t suffer steep losses if wholesale power prices fall.

Under the auction system, the Government sets an overall budget and invites wind farm developers to bid competitively for contracts.

The costs announced today are in addition to subsidies that have previously been awarded to wind, solar and other renewable projects across the UK, which are already adding £2.6bn a year to bills. The latest awards, plus projects already approved but not yet built, will add at least another £3bn by 2031, say Office for Budget Responsibility estimates.

Today’s announcement covered only contracts awarded for offshore wind farms.

Next month will see a separate announcement of the contracts awarded for solar farms, onshore wind and other renewables such as biogas and tidal power.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero will shortly start work on Allocation Round Eight, which is expected to be even larger.

Mr Miliband has argued that such schemes are essential, partly to cut the UK’s greenhouse gas emissions but also because the UK’s declining North Sea oil and gas fields can no longer supply the nation’s needs.

He argues that without renewables, the UK will become increasingly dependent on imports and “petrostates”.

However, the levies needed to subsidise renewables mean Britain has some of the world’s highest energy bills – a key reason why the UK lost two thirds of its manufacturing this century.

‘Disastrous’ for UK

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said cheap power should be the priority and warned that it was a false comparison to suggest electricity from wind farms was cheaper than from gas-fired power stations.

She said: “Our energy system is warped because we’re prioritising decarbonising over everything else. The Conservatives believe we need to return to a system which prioritises cost and energy security. Our cheap power plan would cut electricity bills by 20pc for businesses and households.”

Reform UK also repeated warnings to investors that it would halt any planned renewables projects if it gains power.

Richard Tice, the party’s energy spokesman, said: “For the last 20 years, every expansion of renewable energy has driven power prices higher.

“Electricity under these subsidies will be some 50pc higher price than last year’s average price, and on 20-year inflation-linked contracts. Reform UK is again warning developers, investors and lenders that any new renewables projects will not be recognised as valid.”

“The UK now has among the highest electricity prices in the developed world and cost of living is at the top of voters’ concerns. A further expansion of wind capacity would be highly reckless and would make electricity unaffordable.

“It would be disastrous for the UK economy.”

data.title: Rolls-Royce boss in line for £100m bonus after turnaround success
data.description:
The chief executive of Rolls-Royce is in line for a bonus of £100m or more after spearheading a spectacular turnaround this year.

The payout is the result of 9.3 million shares awarded to Tufan Erginbilgic when he took charge of the engineering giant at the beginning of 2023, tasked with rebuilding the business after its brush with bankruptcy during the pandemic.

At the time, the business’s shares were trading at 91p, valuing the package at £8.4m.

However, Rolls-Royce’s stock has since surged 12-fold as investors’ faith in its transformation plan has snowballed.

With its share price trading at £11.57 on Tuesday [23rd December], the bonus package would be worth £106m if it were cashed in today.

Following his appointment, he described the business as “a burning platform” and said the company had been “grossly mismanaged” before Covid.

However, the Turkish-born British national is now on the cusp of one of the most generous bonus schemes UK plc has ever witnessed after successfully overhauling Rolls-Royce’s fortunes.

His “golden hello” share package vests in two tranches, in 2027 and 2028, ensuring his rescue mission does not run out of steam.

Rolls was one of the FTSE 100’s strongest performers again in 2025, almost doubling its share price over the course of the year as its resurgence gained momentum. Its market capitalisation stands at £97bn.

Mr Erginbilgic took home £13.6m in pay and bonuses in 2023, making him the third-highest-paid boss in the blue-chip index, behind AstraZeneca’s Pascal Soriot and Erik Engstrom, chief executive of publishing and information house Relx.

The bulk of Mr Erginbilgic’s remuneration that year stemmed from a separate share grant worth £7.5m that he received for “remuneration forfeited from previous employment”.

He was previously a partner at the private equity firm Global Infrastructure Partners.

In defending the scheme, Rolls-Royce said at the time that “private equity remuneration arrangements” are “fundamentally different from listed companies”. Last year, his total pay package dropped sharply to £4.1m.

Mr Erginbilgic arrived with Rolls-Royce, still recovering from a vicious tailspin.

The closure of the aviation market in 2020 in response to Covid left it battling to stay afloat. Thousands of job cuts, together with a £2bn emergency cash call under predecessor Warren East, ensured the company’s survival.

Mr Erginbilgic has continued to wield the axe, reining in costs, shelving underperforming contracts, paying down debt, and negotiating better terms with suppliers. A revival in the aviation market has also boosted it.

In 2023, the company set a target of quadrupling operating profit to between £2.5bn and £2.8bn by 2027. The following year, Rolls announced it would distribute £700 in shares to each of its 42,000 employees.

data.title: Student housing giant scraps London development after visa crackdown
data.description:
A student housing giant has abandoned plans to build hundreds of flats in central London after a crackdown on visas for overseas graduates.

Unite, which is Britain’s biggest student landlord, has scrapped the £147m canal-side scheme in Paddington despite securing planning permission last year.

It said the project, consisting of 605 bedrooms, was no longer “financially viable” as it would make insufficient profit for the developer.

The move follows tougher visa requirements for international students coming to the UK as Labour pushes to cut immigration.

Under changes announced last year, universities face a levy on income from international student fees, and tighter restrictions on how long students can stay and work in the UK after they graduate.

Joe Lister, the Unite boss, said there had been a “greater focus” from universities on attracting UK undergraduates, which was changing the landscape.

He said universities had a “more cautious approach” and had delayed signing new lettings contracts while they take stock of their numbers ahead of the UCAS application deadline later this month.

“We’re seeing the impact of the financial pressures on universities – they are less willing to take on financial risk. They just don’t want to be left with void rooms that they have to pay for,” he said.

Unite has also pushed back delivery of a 500-bedroom project in Bristol, saying it needed to explore “options to secure best value”.

Student demand is showing signs of a slowdown. Unite said it has sold 64pc of beds so far for the next academic year, down from 67pc during the previous year.

Rival landlord Empiric Student Property, which Unite is in the process of buying in a £634m takeover, said in November it had experienced a slowdown in demand from Chinese students.

Duncan Garood, the chief executive, said at the time that geopolitical tensions had played a part in this decline.

It comes as figures issued by the Home Office this week showed that net migration was on course to hit a 20-year low, with the number of foreign workers coming to the UK dropping by more than 100,000 in a year during 2024.

While student and seasonal worker applicants stayed flat, the number of dependents fell.

The value of Unite’s properties has slipped, with its main student housing fund down 0.7pc to £2.8bn.

Unite is using the money saved from less construction to fund a £100m share buyback.

data.title: The German plot to woo Rolls-Royce
data.description:
Sitting in front of an enormous prototype jet engine, a German minister beams at the camera as he signs a cooperation deal with two top Rolls-Royce executives.

“With this, both sides commit to actively shape the transformation of aviation,” a social media post boasted gleefully afterwards.

Dietmar Woidke, the president of the German state of Brandenburg, had every reason to feel cheerful at November’s signing ceremony.

In the past decade alone, his state has attracted hundreds of millions of pounds in investment from Rolls and positioned itself as a “centre of excellence” for small-business jet engines.

Now it is jockeying for an even bigger prize – and a chance to swipe some of Britain’s industrial crown jewels.

In recent months, German officials have mounted a charm offensive to secure more work related to Rolls’ next-generation jet engine, the UltraFan.

Rolls-Royce Dahlewitz hub, outside Berlin, has already played a key role in developing what is arguably the new turbofan’s key innovation: its gearbox.

But the Germans are understood to be pushing hard for Dahlewitz to play an even larger role in the future, based on an offer of potentially generous subsidies.

The timing of these lobbying efforts is not coincidental either, coming as Berlin scrambles to defend itself from an avalanche of Chinese cars that threaten to crush Germany’s once-unstoppable motor industry.

Worryingly for Sir Keir Starmer, Rolls appears to be listening.

While the company is understood to favour keeping development in Britain, insiders insist it is duty-bound to consider all options – particularly as it is accountable to shareholders, not the Labour Government in London.

One well-placed industry source said: “The Germans are already involved in UltraFan. The only question now is what they do beyond that.”

Such a loss of investment by the company, widely regarded as a national champion, would deal a serious blow to Starmer’s industrial strategy and risk the loss of valuable know-how to foreign factories.

“It’s a very sensible thing for Rolls-Royce to consider – and potentially very bad for the UK,” warns Rian Whitton, an industry analyst at Bismarck Analysis.

The German advances have come at a critical moment, just as Rolls is considering a return to the fast-growing market for “narrow-body” planes, which have a single aisle and are used by carriers such as Ryanair for short-haul flights.

Rolls-Royce stopped producing narrowbody engines more than a decade ago. Embarrassingly, this meant the company completely missed a boom in demand for best-selling aircraft such as the Boeing 737 and Airbus A320.

But the company’s UltraFan demonstrator offers a route back in, thanks to the engine’s scalability.

Under the leadership of Tufan Erginbilgic, the company is preparing to spend £3bn on developing a narrowbody UltraFan demonstrator.

Rolls is understood to be seeking hundreds of millions of pounds in subsidies for the programme and has raised the issue at the highest levels of Government.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, have visited the company’s Derby headquarters multiple times and used its factory as a backdrop for major announcements.

UltraFan could be a game-changer for several reasons, including improving overall efficiency by around 10pc compared to today’s engines. Engineers believe the design can be scaled up or down, meaning it could be used to power both narrow and so-called “wide-body” aircraft, which have two aisles between the seats.

Insiders acknowledge that the technology improvements also have clear potential applications in defence, such as increasing the capacity or flight time of air tankers, or enabling more compact fighter jet engines.

At a time of global instability and questions about the reliability of the US, such sovereign capabilities are seen as increasingly important.

Still, Germany has already played a crucial role in the development of the first UltraFan demonstrator.

Dahlewitz engineers co-created the power gearbox in a joint venture with domestic company Aerospace Transmission Technologies.

At the same time, Dahlewitz is where Rolls develops and manufactures the Pearl range of business jet engines and is developing new, even smaller engines for “loyal wingman” drones.

However, state and federal government officials are urging Rolls to consider assigning the German site a role in other parts of UltraFan as well.

That would be controversial in Britain, with sites such as Derby currently responsible for designing and developing sections, including the fans, core and combustion chamber.

Within these specialisms lie some of the most valuable and closely guarded secrets in the British aerospace industry.

Jobs in this field are also high-value and distributed geographically across the UK, with any loss of production to foreign factories likely to be politically controversial.

“These jobs are a relatively small component of overall employment, but they are disproportionately high productivity,” Bismarck’s Whitton adds.

“They are important to exports and research and development spending, so they should arguably be prioritised over measures that just increase employment more generally.”

‘Aviation is becoming an important offset’

But Germany is equally desperate to pull them in, as its traditional strongholds are increasingly threatened by a flood of cheap goods from China, says Sander Tordoir, chief economist at the Centre for European Reform.

“German chemicals and carmaking, which is still Germany’s most important industry, are really in deep trouble,” he says.

“So aviation is becoming an important offset – it’s a sector that’s actually growing.”

According to the German Aerospace Industries Association, the number of aerospace jobs rose to 120,000 in 2024, up from around 105,000 a decade earlier.

The country’s aviation industry has long been anchored by Airbus, which conducts final assembly of some aircraft there and also manufactures fuselage sections, cabins, and wing parts domestically.

Germany is also home to Rolls-Royce’s power systems business, which manufactures diesel and gas generators, as well as engines for ships and trains, among other things.

But the country currently lacks a champion capable of making complete commercial jet engines – a weakness that has left it exposed in a row with France over who will get to make what for a next-generation warplane.

This might also partially explain why German officials are so keen to build up this particular muscle, along with the threat from China and the high value of aviation jobs, says Tordoir.

“Airbus is a German-British-French-Spanish collaboration and has done very well. But of course, that’s not how politicians will think of it in the short run,” he adds.

Certainly, some alarm bells should be ringing in Whitehall, according to Bismarck’s Whitton.

But he questions whether ministers and their officials are prepared to act, despite Rolls having recently secured substantial work funded by UK taxpayers.

Rolls was recently chosen by the Government to design the country’s first mini nuclear power plants and has separately won orders for specialist military reactors that will be needed for the Royal Navy’s Dreadnought and Aukus-class submarines.

But in some ways, the fact that Rolls is the sole supplier of some of these technologies gives the Government less leverage in the dispute, not more.

There is also little cash available for handouts, given the constrained state of public finances, whereas in Germany, the government is committing significant spending to defence and infrastructure.

“I think in France, this just wouldn’t happen,” says Whitton. “In part because [national aviation champion] Dassault is family-run and has significant state ownership.

“But unfortunately, Whitehall does not have a tremendously strong hand to play here.

“We talk about Rolls-Royce as a British crown jewel, but that’s got to be heavily caveated. Today it is a fairly globalised, export-led, publicly traded company that does not have a primary loyalty to the British state.”

Still, he argues ministers should fight hard to keep these jobs here.

“They need to have a tough conversation with them,” he says.

The Government has so far attempted to dismiss such concerns. A spokesman said the UK has one of the most competitive aerospace sectors in the world and that it values “the role Rolls-Royce continues to play, supporting thousands of high-quality, high-wage jobs”.

Yet with Germany on Britain’s tail, ministers may soon wish they had paid more attention.

A Rolls-Royce spokesman said the company had a “long-standing relationship with the State of Brandenburg” and looked forward to “building on our successful collaboration in the future”.

data.title: Labour’s economic mismanagement is stifling Britain’s building boom
data.description:
Steve Reed, Labour’s newish housing minister, has certainly brought fresh energy to the role. This has even extended to waving Maga-style caps with Trump-like calls to “build, baby, build!” But while it is still early days, little has improved on the ground.

Britain’s struggling construction sector is worth watching closely for at least three reasons.

First, construction activity is a significant chunk of the UK economy, accounting for about 6pc of GDP. This share has remained relatively stable over decades, even as contributions from manufacturing and agriculture have declined.

The sector also directly employs just over two million people, but this is the lowest since the late 1990s – and workforce shortages are a critical constraint.

Second, the output of the sector provides many other social and economic benefits, including the supply of vital services and potential improvements in productivity.

New housebuilding represents around a quarter of the UK’s productivity total, with another fifth coming from the repair and maintenance of existing homes. The rest is split between infrastructure spending and other public investment, and private industrial and commercial projects.

Third, the performance of the sector has become a bellwether of the success, or otherwise, of the Government’s missions in several areas.

Most obviously, Labour’s 2024 manifesto promised to “get Britain building again” with 1.5 million new homes in England over the course of a five-year parliament. This amounts to an average of around 300,000 a year (or more realistically, 200,000 in the early years, ramping up to 400,000 in the later ones).

In addition, the Government has promised to increase public investment substantially compared to previous plans. And the willingness of private companies to commit to new factories, offices and other commercial spaces is an acid test of confidence in the wider economy.

Unfortunately, then, the latest omens are not good.

The S&P Global UK Construction Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI), which tracks changes in total industry activity, was just 40.1 in December 2025.

This was a marginal improvement on the 39.4 in November but remained below the neutral 50.0 level for the 12th successive month, and still the second-lowest reading since the pandemic in 2020.

Worryingly, the recent weakness in the PMI survey has been broadly spread across housing activity, civil engineering, and commercial construction.

This has not yet been reflected in the official data. However, the latest hard numbers on housing starts and completions remain well below the levels needed to meet the Government’s 1.5 million target.

So, what has gone wrong?

The weakness in UK construction partly reflects headwinds which are also seen in other countries, including heightened geopolitical uncertainty, higher input costs and past increases in interest rates.

Indeed, the construction PMI for the euro area has been below the neutral 50.0 level for even longer – 44 months in total.

Activity might therefore pick up of its own accord as these headwinds fade and especially if UK interest rates continue to fall.

Conditions are already looking a little brighter in the rest of Europe, where borrowing costs have dropped further and more quickly.

The additional drag from pre-Budget uncertainty in the UK may simply have delayed the recovery in construction here by a few months.

Consistent with this, the latest PMI survey found that firms have become more optimistic about the outlook for the year ahead. Some respondents also expressed hopes that 2026 would see an increase in new work in the utilities sector, notably water and energy infrastructure.

Nonetheless, expectations are still weak by past standards, both in the PMI survey and in other polls. 2026 is generally predicted to be better for the construction sector than 2025, but industry forecasts for growth this year are typically being revised down.

This is doubly disappointing because Labour has announced some initiatives which have been widely welcomed.

These include a new initiative, known as the National Planning Policy Framework which, among other things, should make it easier to build housing around railway stations in the “green belt”.

The introduction of the Planning and Infrastructure Act should also reduce delays in housebuilding and speed the construction of new infrastructure, including reservoirs and electricity connections.

But Labour’s “build, baby, build” mission is still struggling to launch.

Despite the best intentions of some in central government, there are plenty of obstacles at the local level, as well as disproportionate costs imposed by bodies such as the new Building Safety Regulator. Rules on everything from biodiversity to parking spaces are still making developments less attractive.

Private developers are also being deterred by obligations to include “affordable” housing. These rules essentially require developers to provide some properties at below market rates, making any project less profitable and reducing the supply of new homes of all kinds.

Meanwhile, even ugly multi-storey car parks are being protected from redevelopment by new “heritage rules”.

The Government’s attempts to ramp up public investment have also been patchy. More generally, confidence is low – among households, businesses and investors, not just in the construction sector. Political and economic uncertainty is increasing again, and Labour’s constant about-turns on other policies are not helping.

As for housing, it is hard enough to sell existing homes, let alone have the confidence to build new ones.

Here, the construction industry may have to rely more on repair and maintenance, including energy efficiency and fire safety work.

This is heavily dependent on government subsidies and, in any event, will not help Labour hit the 1.5 million-home target.

Overall, housing and infrastructure are two areas where the Government is doing at least some of the right things. But Labour’s mismanagement of the rest of the economy is crushing the “animal spirits” that drive growth – including in the construction sector.

Julian Jessop is an independent economist
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour’s rent reforms ‘will cost commercial landlords £11bn’
data.description:
Labour’s crackdown on rental fees charged by high-street landlords could wipe £11bn off the value of shops and offices, a former Treasury economist has warned.

Martin Beck, who worked at the Treasury between 2001 and 2012, said a forthcoming shake-up of rental rules could lead to a 15pc property price slump and billions of pounds of losses for commercial landlords who own sites.

Ministers plan to abolish contracts that include so-called “upwards-only” rental clauses. These allow landlords to only increase rent owed by tenants and not reduce it.

The Government hopes that scrapping the clauses will allow hard-pressed high-street retailers to negotiate rent reductions when leases come up for review.

However, Mr Beck, who is now chief economist at WPI Strategy, has warned the move could backfire.

“The Government is right to want to protect small businesses from rising fixed costs, but it has overlooked a serious downside of how this policy is drafted,” he said.

“By applying a blanket ban on upward-only rent reviews across all commercial property, it risks undermining the very investment that funds town-centre regeneration, data centres and mixed-use developments.

“That could knock billions off property values, weaken pension returns and make many regeneration schemes simply stack up no longer.”

Upward-only rent reviews have been the status quo for Britain’s property industry for decades.

They protect landlords from falling property values or economic downturns by ensuring rents can be raised or remain unchanged, but never decline.

However, they are controversial because tenants may be required to pay above-market rents.

The property industry was blindsided by the Government last year when it published its English devolution bill, which included a provision to ban clauses in leases that specify that rents can only increase, not decrease, when they are renewed.

Ministers had not consulted the industry on their plans before putting them in the bill, prompting a backlash.

Mr Beck warned that the proposals could also harm Britain’s pension funds, which hold about £96bn in commercial property, as the value of their portfolios would fall.

The Prime Minister’s ambition to grow the economy through a building boom could also be undermined because the proposed changes would damage major projects such as data centres and regeneration schemes, said Mr Beck.

Investments by pension funds, local authorities, and other backers in such projects are traditionally made on the basis that modest long-term increases in rental income will repay their contributions.

In 2004, Sir Tony Blair’s government considered proposals to ban upward-only rent review clauses but stopped short of scrapping them.

A spokesman for the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “Upward only rent clauses price small businesses out of our high streets and make companies vulnerable to economic shocks.

“Our English Devolution and Community Empowerment Bill will abolish these outdated clauses in new and renewed leases, so we can boost job opportunities and unlock growth”.

data.title: Miliband’s wife set to lose battle to stop new £5m flats
data.description:
Ed Miliband’s wife and the actor Benedict Cumberbatch are set to fail in their attempt to prevent a new residential development from being built close to their north London homes.

Plans submitted by HGG London propose the demolition of a 1930s house and its replacement with a building containing five flats in Dartmouth Park, near Camden.

The application attracted significant opposition from residents, who argued that the scale of the project would harm neighbouring properties.

Objectors raised concerns about loss of light and privacy, as well as increased traffic and noise, and said the scheme represented an “over-development” of the site.

But council officers have recommended the scheme for approval.

Dame Justine Thornton, the wife of the Secretary of State for Energy Security and Net Zero, and Cumberbatch were responsible for two of the 205 objections during the consultation process. The council received just three letters of support.

A report prepared for the council by BPS Chartered Surveyors estimated that the completed flats would have a combined value of more than £5m, with individual units expected to sell for between £700,000 and £1.4m.

Planning officers concluded that the proposal would make better use of previously developed land and contribute to housing delivery in line with planning policy.

In a report to Camden council’s planning committee, council officers said: “The proposed development does not constitute overdevelopment but instead reflects an appropriate and carefully considered density for this well-connected, underutilised site. 

“The proposals have been designed to avoid an overbearing impact on neighbours.

“The level and nature of the harm has been carefully considered and viewed in the context of the fact that the development comes with increased density which would deliver five homes, four of which would be suitable for families for which there is a great need. Given the council’s current housing delivery position, significant weight should be given to the housing delivery.”

According to the report, two councillors also expressed concerns about the plans.

Dame Justine, who was a barrister for 25 years before becoming a judge, submitted a formal objection in which she described the design as “too tall, too bulky and too dense given the context of the surrounding houses and the wider conservation area”.

In the same submission, she said she had “no objection to the principle of development, particularly in the context of the need for more housing”.

Mr Cumberbatch and his wife, the opera director Sophie Hunter, also objected to the plans.

In representations seen by The Telegraph, they expressed concern that the development would encourage further construction in the area and said it would “disrupt the aesthetic of the street” and undermine its architectural character.

Other residents highlighted the inclusion of external heat pumps, warning that they could generate persistent noise. One neighbour, Ruth Liebling, said the equipment would cause “constant noise pollution to nearby properties”.

Last year, Miliband’s own department said research it commissioned found that noise complaints from heat pumps were not common and that heat pumps were largely thought to be quiet.

Design ‘out of scale’ with surrounding homes

The Dartmouth Park Neighbourhood Forum, which represents local residents, said the proposed building was “out of scale” with surrounding homes and disproportionately tall.

Ms Thornton’s intervention comes despite repeated criticism by the Prime Minister of what he has described as “nimbys” and “blockers” preventing new houses from being built. Sir Keir has previously described himself as a “yimby”, meaning ‘yes in my backyard’.

In a statement to the council, the developer HGG London said: “The development is an excellent opportunity to improve the economic, social, and environmental conditions of the area, and it is in accordance with the development policies on housing.”

The company also cited Labour’s updated planning framework, which states that brownfield housing schemes should be approved unless they cause substantial harm.

Camden Council abandoned a decision for councillors to rubberstamp the plans on Thursday due to technical issues.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Spain train crash ‘caused by broken track’ months after £600m renovation
data.description:
Experts investigating the cause of a high-speed train crash in Spain that killed 39 people have found a broken joint on the rails, a source has revealed.

The tail end of a train heading from Malaga to Madrid derailed at 7.45pm on Sunday, smashing into an oncoming train travelling from the capital to Huelva.

Investigators at the scene identified some wear on the joint between sections of the rail, known as a fishplate, which they said showed the fault had been there for some time, a source briefed on the investigations told Reuters.

They ‍found that the ‌faulty joint created a gap between the rail sections that widened as trains continued to travel along the track, which was renovated in May as part of a €700m (£608m) project.

The first carriages of the train that derailed, operated by Spanish firm Iryo, drove over the gap in ‌the rails. But the eighth, ‍and last, carriage derailed, bringing with it the seventh and sixth carriages, the source said.

Authorities are yet to establish an official cause of the crash, with Oscar Puente, the Spanish transport minister, calling the collision “truly strange”.

The Iryo train was less than four years old and passed an inspection on Jan 15.

Alvaro Fernandez Heredia, the president of Renfe, the operator of the second train, ruled out the possibility of human error or excessive speed.

The speed limit on the track is 250km/h, while the trains were travelling at 210km/h and 205km/h when they collided.

In the months leading up to the crash, Spain’s railway infrastructure manager Adif warned of at least eight technical incidents on the high-speed line.

Most of the problems related to the signalling system, although there were also issues related to overhead power lines and other infrastructure.

The government was asked about the issues last summer and responded by saying it had addressed two “technical incidents affecting the signalling systems”.

‘Rare and difficult to explain’

In an official statement, Iryo said it “deeply regretted what had happened” and that it had activated “all its emergency protocols”.

Authorities said there were 20 seconds between the Iryo train veering off the track and the subsequent collision, leaving the driver of the second train – who was killed in the crash – with almost no time to react before the impact.

Mr Heredia said the signalling and track-based safety mechanisms designed to detect an occupied track had no time to activate.

He also warned that determining the exact cause of the crash could take several days and that the circumstances were “rare and difficult to explain”.

The investigation is being led by the Commission for the Investigation of Railway Accidents, which operates within Spain’s transport ministry.

A separate judicial investigation has also been launched by a court in Montoro, Cordoba.

Juanma Moreno, Andalucia’s regional president, said: “It is likely [that more dead will be found] when you look at the mass of metal that is there. The firefighters have done a great job, but unfortunately, when they get the heavy machinery to lift the carriages, it is probable we will find more victims.

“Here at ground zero, when you look at this mass of twisted iron, you see the violence of the impact.”

Mr Moreno said that authorities were also searching the area nearby.

“The impact was so incredibly violent that we have found bodies hundreds of meters away,” he added.

Video released by Spain’s Guardia Civil showed the sides of the worst-hit carriages shredded open, with train seats lying on the surrounding gravel.

One carriage lay on its side, bent around a large concrete pillar, with debris scattered around the area.

“There were moments when we had to remove the dead to get to the living,” Francisco Carmona, firefighter chief of Cordoba, told Onda Cero radio.

Following the crash, a sports centre in Adamuz was turned into a makeshift hospital, and the Spanish Red Cross established a help centre for emergency service workers and people seeking information.

“The scene was horrific. It was terrible,” Rafael Moreno, the mayor of Adamuz, told reporters. “People asking and begging for help. Those leaving the wreckage. Images that will always stay in my mind.”

Spanish King Felipe VI expressed his condolences, adding that the royal house was looking to visit Adamuz in the coming days.

“I understand the desperation of the families and the number of injured people who have suffered this accident, and we are all really worried,” he said.

On Monday, the Spanish flag was flown at half-mast in front of Madrid’s Parliament, while Pedro Sanchez, the country’s prime minister, visited the crash site.

Spain has spent decades investing in high-speed trains and currently has the largest rail network in Europe for trains over 155 mph, with more than 1,900 miles of track, according to the European Union.

Sunday’s crash was the first with deaths on a high-speed train since Spain’s high-speed rail network opened its first line in 1992.
