data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: Wind farm championed by Starmer axed after Miliband snub
data.description:
A wind farm backed by BP has been scrapped and the fate of a second left uncertain after failing to land government contracts in Ed Miliband’s latest round of renewables subsidies.

The developer said one of the Irish Sea offshore wind projects, known as Morgan, was no longer viable after missing out on taxpayer support during the latest auction.

The project was one of two backed by BP and German energy utility EnBW. However, EnBW has now pulled out, leaving BP’s joint venture to progress the second wind farm, Mona, alone.

EnBW said: “The primary reason for EnBW’s withdrawal is that the two projects did not receive government support.”

That is despite Sir Keir Starmer having championed the sites last year, claiming they were proof of the UK’s commitment to green energy.

EnBW said the lack of subsidies meant the projects were “no longer economically viable”, citing higher supply chain costs and steeper interest rates.

Its decision risks dashing hopes for £100m of planned investment in the projects and putting hundreds of jobs in Scotland and Belfast at risk.

BP has already stepped away from both schemes, folding its interested into a joint venture with Japanese utility firm JERA called JERA Nex bp.

On Friday evening, JERA Nex bp announced that it would scrap the Morgan wind farm in the Irish Sea, handing licences back to the Crown Estate, but that it would attempt to progress the Mona project.

This will be now depend on winning subsidies in the next round of renewable awards.

Mr Miliband rejected the developers’ bid for subsidies during the latest auction of renewable energy contracts known as Allocation Round 7.

The Mona and Morgan projects were to lie roughly halfway between Blackpool and the Isle of Man, with a total of nearly 200 giant 15-megawatt turbines, each standing about 1,200ft tall.

Planning for the schemes was well advanced.

Both wind farms had secured government planning consent, and the joint venture partners had leased Belfast Harbour to build the turbines.

If successful, the remaining Mona project would deliver 300 jobs across sites in Northern Ireland and Scotland.

Cancellation will be a blow to local communities and raise awkward questions for some members of the Government.

Hilary Benn, Northern Ireland secretary, visited Belfast Harbour last November to hold talks with executives from EnBW and JERA Nex BP.

At the time, he said: “Having met with the joint venture partners and seen the scale of the development work at Belfast Harbour, it’s clear that this deal will secure Northern Ireland’s key role in the UK’s clean energy future.”

Sir Keir Starmer also highlighted the two projects prior to his visit to the UN climate conference in Brazil, claiming they were integral to the Government’s “mission to bring about the clean power revolution”.

A DESNZ spokesman said: “We are pleased that Jera Nex BP will continue its development of the Mona project, which will mean good jobs and investment into Belfast Harbour.

“Any decision by a shareholder to stop investment in a project is a commercial matter and discussions on alternative solutions for the Morgan project are ongoing.

“Our mission does not rely on individual projects and we are confident our pipeline of projects will deliver clean power by 2030 and get bills down for good.”

BP was contacted for comment.

data.title: Drivers could be fined years after speeding
data.description:
Drivers face being fined years after breaking the speed limit under Labour’s road safety plans.

Ministers are planning to remove the six-month time limit on legal action for speeding offences, which has resulted in thousands of cases being cancelled.

Under the plans, the six-month time limit will only start when police have been informed who was driving a vehicle that broke the speed limit or went through a red light, which can sometimes take months.

The Government argues that the requirement for alleged offenders to receive a court summons within six months is “open to abuse and delaying tactics” and “can result in offenders avoiding justice”.

If a driver contests a fine, some alleged offenders can wait 18 months for a magistrates’ hearing because of court backlogs.

But Nick Freeman, one of Britain’s top motoring lawyers, told The Telegraph that the proposed new system would only result in further delays, and that some motorists accused of speeding could wait years for the result of legal action.

He said the Government’s plan was a “lame excuse to cover for their own inefficiencies”, arguing that “the only people abusing this system are the prosecuting authorities”.

“There has to be a time limitation,” he said. “The purpose of this legislation is so that people can genuinely remember who was driving the vehicle, but the problem is the authorities are not efficient. They need to get their act together.”

Mr Freeman, who runs his own legal firm and is known as “Mr Loophole” for his success in defending celebrities convicted of driving offences, said further delays would result in slower justice for innocent people.

“We don’t want people to be prosecuted for a red light offence three years later,” he said.

The current system requires police to issue a suspect with a Section 172 notice, which requires them to confirm who was driving the vehicle at the time of the alleged offence.

Within 28 days, the owner of the vehicle must then confirm that they were driving at the time, or give police details of the person who was behind the wheel. The second person is then given a further 28 days to respond.

After receiving the information, police are then required to ask a magistrates’ court to issue a summons. All stages of the process must be completed within six months for the prosecution to continue, and courts often issue a summons on the final day of that period.

The Government does not routinely publish data on the number of speeding prosecutions abandoned by prosecutors, although analysis by the RAC Foundation in 2022 found that 17 per cent were scrapped for a variety of reasons, including that prosecutors exceeded the six-month limit.

Speeding offences are usually punished with a fine as a percentage of a driver’s weekly income, up to a maximum of £1,000. Offenders can also be ordered to participate in speed awareness courses.

Labour’s road safety plan also includes a plan to give people who drive without wearing a seatbelt three points on their driving licence, in addition to the current penalty of a fine. The rule will also apply to passengers under the age of 14, who are legally the responsibility of the driver.

Ministers have already been criticised for the plan to lower drink-driving limits, which hospitality bosses said will reduce footfall in rural pubs.

The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save Britain’s pubs after a series of measures by Labour left thousands of businesses questioning whether they would be able to stay afloat.

In response, Lillian Greenwood, the road safety minister, said the new rules were not designed to prevent the public “having a great night out”.

“What we’re just saying is don’t take your car,” she told Times Radio. “So that might mean that, you know, some places you’ll be able to take a bus or a taxi. In other places, you’re going out with a group of mates, one of you agrees to be the designated driver.”

A Department for Transport spokeswoman said: “This consultation will seek all views before any decisions are made.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The towns where the taps ran dry
data.description:
On a grey Tuesday in East Grinstead, a little girl runs along the pavement to the redbrick town library. She stands at the automatic glass doors waiting for them to open, but nothing happens. A moment later her mother arrives. The woman looks at the sign that reads “library closed today”, then tells the child she will not be able to borrow any books after all.

Like almost every business or public building in this West Sussex town, the library is shut as a result of South East Water’s failure to keep the taps running.

The company’s bosses might just about be forgiven if this were a one-off. But it is the second officially declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent, still facing disruption after weeks of problems.

South East has repeatedly fallen short on its most basic duty. In 2023, 6,000 households in Kent and Sussex went without water when supplies at South East ran out. In 2022, thousands of people spent Christmas with no water due to leakages.

The company, which serves 2.3 million customers, is now well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from basket-case Thames Water, leaving its licence to operate under threat.

Businesses on the brink

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, who took home £515,000 last year including a £115,000 bonus, was hauled in front of a parliamentary committee earlier this month. He had been summoned to answer questions on the crisis that hit Tunbridge Wells in December and ruined Christmas for 24,000 homes and businesses.

The spa town made an unlikely backdrop for a water crisis. From the 17th century, Tunbridge Wells’s mineral waters drew bathers and wealth.

But in the 21st century, water dealt an economic blow. Pub and bar owners in Tunbridge Wells told The Telegraph they had lost ruinous amounts of money.

“In four days we lost between £15,000 to £20,000. We are really struggling,” Bianca Omer, the manager of the cocktail bar Rocca said.

The bar survived. But Tudor Price, the chief executive of Kent’s chamber of commerce, believes that pubs, restaurants and bars in Tunbridge Wells “potentially lost about £20m of revenue” as a result of the outage.

Some are now on the brink. With many pubs already threatened by the Chancellor’s business rates raid, lengthy closure may deliver the final blow for some in Tunbridge Wells.

More alarmingly, a kidney treatment centre, 19 care homes, and two hospitals were left without water too.

‘Flying blind’

Debate has raged over whether the crisis in Tunbridge Wells was foreseeable. Hinton, perhaps unsurprisingly, reckons not.

At his parliamentary hearing he told Alistair Carmichael, the no-nonsense MP for Orkney and chairman of the environment select committee, that the whole thing was a bolt from the blue. In fact, because the crisis came as such a shock, Hinton insisted, he’d give himself “eight out of 10 in terms of response”.

The A-grade mark, as Carmichael put it, raised eyebrows among committee members. But when Marcus Rink, the boss of the Drinking Water Inspectorate, delivered his verdict, the benefit of the doubt evaporated.

“It shouldn’t have been a surprise,” Rink said. The water treatment works at nearby Pembury hadn’t just gone down for a few hours – it had been failing for some time, he explained.

“The start of the incident, in the opinion of my inspectors, was Nov 9.” With the Pembury plant the only water treatment works serving Tunbridge Wells, its continued good health is essential to the area.

Rink said South East was “flying blind”. The plant lacked electronic monitoring, relying on manual observations that missed failing filters.

Despite prior regulatory enforcement notices and missing micro-filtration units, the company had ignored weeks of visible deterioration, eventually leading to a totally avoidable system collapse.

The picture for South East became worse as Rink laid out more details. The company had been notified that there were issues with the treatment works a year previously. Testing had been intermittent, and the specialists monitoring the facility had all knocked off on Friday afternoon before returning on Monday morning. Nov 9 was a Sunday.

If MPs have been determined to hold Hinton to account, the same cannot be said of South East Water’s non-executive directors. Other managers have been sent out to face the music, among them a flustered Neil Hudson, South East’s head of water quality.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” a local BBC news reporter demanded to know after residents were advised to boil their water before using it.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impact whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson said.

Asked by MPs why he had dodged the media, Hinton claimed journalists would not have asked relevant questions. They would have focused on his salary and South East Water’s finances, he complained.

When he finally came out of hiding, Hinton told ITV that he was “an operational CEO” and as such had spent the crisis participating in “traditional CEO activities”.

Henry Tuffnell, the young Labour MP for Pembrokeshire who sits on the environment committee, pointed out that for an “operational CEO” Hinton seemed to be remarkably short of operational detail.

Tuffnell tells The Telegraph: “You can’t say you were all over the detail, and therefore didn’t have time to do any media, if you weren’t over the detail.”

Even as Hinton faced MPs, back in Tunbridge Wells a second major incident was already in the offing. Still recovering from the pre-Christmas outage, locals were cut off again on Saturday.

Round two, according to South East, was due to “recent cold weather and a subsequent breakout of leaks and bursts”. This was no minor blip. Within days, 30,000 homes and businesses across Kent and Sussex had no water at all.

‘We have had enough’

In East Grinstead, four schoolboys kick a yellow football against the wall. They are the only people in the town who seem to think the outage is a good thing. “It’s a day off,” one of them offers cheerfully.

In Waitrose, almost all of the water has gone from the shelves except for a few bottles of San Pellegrino and some strawberry-flavoured Volvic. There was a water station in the car park the previous day, but it closed after 90 minutes.

East Grinstead council said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town. To the best of the council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations.

“The town council regrets that it was not involved from the outset, as early engagement and local knowledge could have helped ensure a smoother, safer, and more coordinated response for the town’s residents.”

Local knowledge is clearly not South East Water’s strong suit. In December the company began its comedy of errors in Tunbridge Wells with the opening of a water station in Tonbridge, seven miles away.

For those affected, it’s hard to see the funny side. Gary, who lives homeless in East Grinstead, and had just two bottles of water left, told The Telegraph he was “scared”. Like many in the town, he wondered: “What is this all about?”

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident who set up the campaign group Dry Wells Action, says: “We can’t wash, flush toilets, do the dishes, wash clothes, top up boilers – all the normal stuff you take for granted – because of this abject failure by South East Water.

“We have had enough. We will now fight for our right to water. Tunbridge Wells is no longer disgusted. We are absolutely furious.”

Heavy hitters

South East’s board is now facing serious questions. Chris Train, the chairman, has been no more forthcoming about appearing publicly than his chief executive.

Described on the company’s website as bringing “a lot of experience”, he is also chairman of Sellafield, which in 2024 was fined for serious cyber security failings. The Commons public accounts committee criticised the nuclear power plant last year for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns”.

After Hinton’s select committee mauling, Train offered a review by one of the non-executive directors.

Committee chairman Alistair Carmichael responded: “Members of the press and public would be forgiven for seeing Mr Train’s announcement of a further review of the Pembury failure as an attempt to buy themselves time, to hunker down until this storm blows over.

“They also assert that their review will be ‘independent’. It stretches the meaning of ‘independent’ when their review is to be conducted by a member of its board, supported by its staff. What was the investigation done by the Drinking Water Inspectorate if it was not an independent review?”

Hinton’s furious phone call

Desperate Tunbridge Wells residents have called for the Army to be brought in. Though that may sound a little over the top, there is logic to the request.

Hawker says: “The Army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in emergency situations.”

South East Water officials can’t even manage a basic task like carting bottles of water from one place to another, he argues.

If Hinton has been the baddie, Mike Martin, the Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, has emerged as something of a local hero. He has had his sleeves rolled up throughout the process and repeatedly taken Hinton to task. This resulted in Hinton phoning Martin one evening during the eye of the storm to give him both barrels.

In his evidence to the environment committee, Martin said Hinton had accused him of politicising the crisis, asking: “How can you sleep at night?” The chief executive admitted to Carmichael that the telephone tantrum had not been his “finest moment”.

South East is more used to getting away with it.

As well as the four major shortages customers have endured on Hinton’s watch, South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since Hinton took over. In 2023, it had the dubious accolade of recording the most interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Its performance is getting worse, not better. In 2025, just 13 of it 38 annual targets were met – a little more than a third. Among the areas where it fell short were leakages, supply interruptions, water taste and odour, customer satisfaction and value for money. The previous two years, South East scored 15 – equivalent to less than 40pc. In 2022, it ranked positively on 24 of the 38 goals.

The company has suggested that its systems would be much better if only it had been able to raise customers’ bills, a familiar refrain in the water industry.

Yet it already has. Bills went up by 20pc just last year, meaning the average customer now pays £294 a year. It is also one of only five companies pushing to lift bills above the figure set by the regulator over the next five years.

Ofwat also gave short shrift to South East’s most recent investment plan. The company’s performance commitments were “of low to moderate ambition and do not go far enough in some cases”, the regulator said last April.

When it comes to “water supply interruptions, per capita consumption, repairs to burst mains and biodiversity”, the targets South East Water proposed “remained among the lowest across the sector”, Ofwat added.

South East responded by accusing the watchdog of being “unfair, disproportionate and punitive”. The company boasts of having “60 teams repairing leaks reactively and 86 leakage technicians and analysts searching for and finding leaks proactively”. More than 25,000 leaks were repaired in 2024, it says.

For any customers who feel they’re not getting value for money, the company points out: “The average drinking water bill now costs 81p per day, less than the cost of a second-class stamp.”

A spokesman said: “Our priority is improving South East Water in the future. We are strategically focused on our business plan and committed to delivering the infrastructure investment needed. This will improve our resilience as we face the challenges of climate change and population growth.”

It adds that Pembury’s water treatment works at the centre of the Tunbridge Wells crisis is due a full upgrade: “In advance of this full refurbishment, a number of improvements and changes are being made. Some of these are complete, and others are still ongoing, all of which are in line with the regulators’ timescales.”

Finances are underwater

South East’s financial performance is no less wretched than its operational performance. The company has failed to post a profit for the last three years, and though pre-tax losses roughly halved to £19.7m last year, they total £130m since 2022.

Weighed down with £1.3bn of borrowings – a figure that has steadily increased every year under Hinton – the cost of servicing those debts regularly eclipses the company’s profits. In the last five years, more than £400m has gone out of the door on debt costs and finance charges alone.

A further £168m has been paid out dividends, £136m of it in the form of a regulator-approved repayment of an internal loan to its parent company in 2020. South East Water is ultimately owned by a consortium comprising Australian and Canadian funds and NatWest’s pension fund.

In the two-year period from 2020 to 2022, the amount that South East paid out in dividends and debt interest surpassed what it spent upgrading infrastructure.

In 2024, the company revealed that it needed further cash to stay afloat. Ten months later, it received a £200m equity injection from shareholders.

Yet none of this has stopped Hinton receiving hundreds of thousands of pounds in pay and bonuses every year. Total earnings since assuming the top job in 2020 stand at an estimated £2.3m. Last year, he was paid a basic salary of £307,000, a £115,000 bonus and received a further £35,000 in pension contributions and other benefits.

A spokesman for South East said: “South East Water remains committed to a remuneration framework that supports a performance culture, and recognises success but does not reward poor performance.

“The bonus scheme is evaluated by the company’s independent non-executive director-led remuneration committee. The executive directors are not part of this committee and have no say in their remuneration.

“We can confirm that no bonus payment was made for operational performance for the year 2024-25. Details of all payments made can be found in our annual report on our website.”

‘Out of control’

Yet pinning the fiasco on a lack of investment when executives’ backs are against the wall doesn’t wash with Tunbridge Wells MP Mike Martin.

“Quite clearly, corporate governance has broken down,” he said. “We are now in a situation where a major public utility is out of control – failing its customers, risking public health and treating Parliament with contempt.”

If the board won’t step up, Martin thinks the utility’s bosses have to get involved. NatWest’s pension scheme owns 25pc of South East Water.

“NatWest Group Pension Fund will use its influence as a minority shareholder to direct South East Water’s board to ensure these issues are fully resolved,” it said in response to a letter from Martin this week.

The regulator has been spurred to action too. On Wednesday evening, the Government finally showed some mettle by calling on Ofwat to review South East Water’s licence to operate.

In an uncharacteristic show of speediness, on Thursday Ofwat announced an investigation into whether the supplier had met its obligations and offered support to those affected. Ofwat sceptics point out the regulator has been investigating South East since 2023.

There are local suspicions that Labour have, to a degree, let the crisis escalate because Tunbridge Wells and East Grinstead will never turn red. Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, told The Telegraph that’s overthinking it. He said: “The problem is utter management incompetence at South East Water. My hunch is cock-up, not conspiracy.”

Yet the crisis is ongoing, and the inquisition is just getting started. By the water station at East Grinstead sports club, where the cars are backed up as far as you can see and people are pushing shopping trolleys full of bottles back home, a little boy on a bike asks if you can drink the water from the pond.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britons believe illegally streaming sport is socially acceptable
data.description:
The majority of Britons keen on sport believe it is now socially acceptable to use pirate streaming services, according to an authoritative report.

Findings in the Sport Industry Report 2026, a comprehensive snapshot of the UK industry, will concern authorities fighting the rise in illegal streaming.

Despite threats of jail terms, research by the Intellectual Property Office estimated almost four million people in the UK had used an illegal source to watch live sport in 2023. A wide-ranging twin survey of fans and sports professionals conducted by Nielsen Sports suggests broadcaster rip-off services like hacked “Fires Sticks” remain prevalent.

The research says 58 per cent of fans and 66 per cent of professionals “say it is now socially acceptable to use pirate streaming services”. In a temperature check on the commercial future of UK sport, the research also shows 2025 saw $4bn investment into the sector.

Other findings include:

  • “Affordability” is now the number one concern for fans across every age group, with 67 per cent believing attending live sport will be a luxury within the next five years
  • 56 per cent of fans and 70 per cent of professionals view climate change as an existential threat to sport
  • 80 per cent of fans and 92 per cent of professionals support social media restrictions for under-16s
  • 73 per cent of fans would support another London Olympic bid

The data also reveals “growing tension between commercial growth and social responsibility”, the report says.

Across every demographic, “affordability emerged as the single biggest issue facing sport’s future”. Nick Keller, chairman of Sport Industry Group, said: “Today’s research captures a defining contradiction for sport. The industry is confident, yet it is operating in one of the most unstable global environments for generations. Climate disruption, geopolitical tension, AI-driven change, mental-health pressures and social fragmentation are no longer peripheral – they are reshaping how sport operates, competes and grows.

“This report reflects that tension: optimism about sport’s future, paired with a clear understanding that the next phase of growth will demand more intentional choices than the last.”

A sample of 1,002 members of the public who are either “interested” or “very interested” in sport were polled. A total of 264 sports professionals who are either “interested” or “very interested” in sport were also questioned.

Fieldwork took place in October. The report features contributions from organisations including the England and Wales Cricket Board, YouTube, Baller League, Surj, Populous, Stats Perform, Elemis, Personar, Canto, British Gas, Think Beyond and Little Dot Sport.

Andrew Milnes, Market Leader UK & Ireland at Nielsen Sports, said: “There is strong belief in sport’s future, but also a growing expectation that the industry must lead, on climate, on integrity, on access and on fan experience. This research provides a robust evidence base for decision-makers at a critical moment for UK sport.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Debanking soars to record high
data.description:
Have you been debanked? Email money@telegraph.co.uk.

Almost half a million customers were debanked last year, the highest figure in a decade, as account closures continued to spike.

An estimated 453,230 accounts were shut down, according to figures released to The Telegraph under Freedom of Information rules. This was a more than tenfold increase on the 45,091 accounts closed in 2016-17, and an 11pc jump on the 408,000 accounts closed in the 2023-24 tax year.

In all of the cases, lenders cited “financial crime reasons” for the decision to close accounts.

Nigel Farage, who was himself controversially debanked by private bank Coutts in 2023, described the figures as “appalling” and blamed European legislation, which he claimed “makes it cheaper for banks to close accounts over unusual transactions”.

It comes ahead of the introduction of new rules which will force lenders to give customers longer before they close accounts – at least 90 days’ notice – as well as offer clearer explanations of why they shut the accounts.

But the requirements will only apply to accounts opened after April 28 this year and will be subject to exemptions to allow banks to comply with financial crime rules.

Financial institutions are allowed to close accounts for commercial reasons, and if they suspect criminal activity. There is no legal right to a bank account in the UK, unlike in countries such as France and Belgium.

Banks have faced criticism over their failure to explain to customers exactly why accounts have been closed. This is often blamed on anti-money laundering and other financial crime rules which lenders must follow.

Anti-money laundering rules were substantially tightened in 2017, requiring banks to monitor customer activity and check identities. The rules mean that banks which suspect customers are engaging in criminal activity cannot tell them why their accounts are being closed.

The National Crime Agency estimates that £10bn is laundered through the UK’s banking systems every year.

In December, the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) fined Nationwide £44m for ineffective warning systems, and for knowing that some customers were breaching its own terms.

In one case, Nationwide allowed a customer to receive 24 payments totalling £27.3m over 13 months into a personal current account. HMRC was unable to recover £800,000 of the payments.

Since 2021, the city regulator has imposed 13 fines on banks for failing to observe money laundering rules, totalling more than £300m.

Another pressure point for lenders is rules introduced in October 2024 which require them to reimburse victims of authorised push payment (APP) fraud.

In the first half of last year, lenders repaid in 88pc of cases, totalling more than £84m, with the vast majority of refunds paid out within days. Experts previously told The Telegraph that the reimbursement rules could be driving higher numbers of account closures.

James Graham, a senior researcher at think tank the Prosperity Institute, said: “The continued rise in account closures, as shown by this new data, shows that the debanking epidemic remains a present and real danger for increasing numbers of British citizens.

“Preventing crime sounds noble, but nobody believes another 453,000 people are financial criminals, on top of the 408,000 in 2024, and 317,000 in 2023.”

Maxwell Marlow, of think tank the Adam Smith Institute, said: “The scourge of debanking continues to blight the British public and significantly impacts the Square Mile.

“Our finance system thrives through freedom – it is why we were the centre of global capitalism for so long, and we became rich from it. If we choose to reject these principles by ignoring this issue, our liberties and prosperity will be punished collectively.”

A UK Finance spokesman said: “Banks must comply with strict legal and regulatory requirements in terms of verifying customers and preventing financial crime. As a result, a small proportion of accounts are closed, but only after extensive review and investigation.”

A Treasury spokesman said: “Debanking is wrong, which is why we’ve put protections in place for millions of people and small businesses.

“Under the new rules, customers will receive at least 90 days’ notice and an explanation for any account closure, strengthening protections which prohibit banks from discriminating against someone based on their political opinions.”

An FCA spokesman said: “Fighting financial crime is one of our priorities. Fraud makes up over 40 pc of crime in the UK, robbing people of their hard-earned money.

“It’s important banks and building societies play their part, including closing accounts they have suspicions about. Only a tiny fraction of accounts are closed – and we expect firms to act proportionately and treat customers fairly.”

data.title: Bidding war rises over weight loss drug that could prevent ‘Ozempic face’
data.description:
Two of the world’s largest pharmaceutical companies are locked in a bidding war for the maker of a weight-loss drug that could help patients avoid succumbing to “Ozempic face”.

Danish giant Novo Nordisk has launched an attempt to gatecrash Pfizer’s $7.3bn (£5.6bn) takeover of US biotech Metsera by tabling an offer of up to $9bn.

Metsera, which had already agreed to the Pfizer deal, described the approach from Novo Nordisk as “superior”.

However, Pfizer hit back by branding the offer as “reckless”. The US pharmaceutical giant now has four working days to salvage a deal.

It comes as drug giants scramble to secure the newest and most innovative weight-loss treatments to meet soaring demand.

Metsera is developing treatments based on the hormone amylin, which researchers have suggested could stop people from losing muscle during rapid weight loss.

Currently, Eli Lilly’s Mounjaro and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy help people to shed pounds, but also lead them to lose muscle as well as fat.

The same goes for Novo’s Ozempic, which is a diabetes drug used off-label for weight loss.

‘Ozempic face’

As well as being praised for the weight-loss credentials, the drugs have also been blamed for the rise of “Ozempic face”. This is where people receiving treatment start to appear gaunt as they lose muscle mass in their cheeks.

However, researchers have suggested that amylin could help people to maintain muscle even as they lose weight, as the hormone appears to play a role in maintaining body composition.

Dimitris Papamargaritis, an associate professor at the University of Leicester, earlier this year said: “Early lab studies suggest that adding amylin could help people lose more fat while sparing muscle, though this still needs confirmation in humans.”

Pharmaceutical companies have become increasingly aware of the issue as the drugs grow in popularity.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the AstraZeneca chief executive, last year said weight-loss medicines needed to be more targeted towards fat to help people avoid losing too much muscle in the long term.

He said: “Today you lose weight but you lose fat and you lose muscle. The problem with many of [the treatments] is when you stop, you regain weight, because obesity is a chronic disease.

“Most people, as soon as they stop taking the medicines, they regain fat, but not so much the muscle that they have lost unless, of course, they go to the gym.”

AstraZeneca is developing its own weight-loss treatments, with the aim of leapfrogging rivals with new, potentially cheaper, obesity drugs. In 2023, it bought a Chinese biotech which was working on a weight-loss pill.

Metsera is also developing pill-based weight-loss drugs, as well as longer-lasting treatments. It is testing one injection that people would only have to take once a month, compared to current weight-loss jabs that require once-a-week injections.

Novo Nordisk made the offer for the US biotech as it battles a slump in performance, having recently announced plans to cut 9,000 jobs.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Housebuilding giant blames Budget for biggest drop in sales since Covid
data.description:
Britain’s largest housebuilder has suffered the biggest drop in the number of homes sold since the pandemic after buyers were spooked by Rachel Reeves’s Budget.

Vistry, which owns brands such as Bovis and Countryside, sold 9.7pc fewer homes last year with sales falling to 15,700 from 17,225 in 2024.

Greg Fitzgerald, the company’s chief executive, said uncertainty over the November Budget had “delayed the timing” of some sales with “a more subdued market” triggered by Ms Reeves’s tax and spending plans.

The months leading to the Budget were dominated by speculation over the potential property taxes that the Chancellor would bring in to plug a multibillion-pound black hole in the public’s finances.

A so-called mansion tax on expensive homes will hit properties worth more than £2m with a tax surcharge from 2028 onwards.

Vistry’s woes will also prove a headache for Labour as it strives to hit its goal of building 1.5 million homes by the end of this parliament.

The Government has pledged to accelerate building plans to meet the target after a slow start left it behind schedule.

At Vistry, sales for future construction projects by the housebuilder – which teams up with housing associations and councils to build affordable homes – stand at around £4bn, compared with £4.4bn in the previous year.

The sales drop marks the largest decline since the depths of the Covid pandemic, when sales plunged by a third to 4,652 as lockdown wiped out the property market.

Shares in the housebuilder fell by nearly 8pc during trading, wiping off £52m.

However, the builder backed Labour’s affordable housing measures, which include billions of pounds of funding to boost partnership contracts over the next decade, saying it will buoy trading.

Mr Fitzgerald said: “Whilst market conditions remain uncertain in the near term, further benefits of our cost, productivity and mix enhancement initiatives will support the delivery of good year-on-year financial and strategic progress.”

It comes after Persimmon, another housebuilder, warned it did not expect “any material improvement” in the housing market this year despite the Government’s efforts.

The builder warned that inflation will remain high, while extra regulatory costs and landfill tax charges will slow down growth.

data.title: Billionaire former Tory donor pays himself £477m
data.description:
A billionaire former Tory donor paid himself £477m last year as revenues soared at his City hedge fund.

Chris Rokos cemented his position as one of Britain’s richest men, and is believed to be the highest-paid partner at his eponymous fund, Rokos Capital Management, which more than doubled its turnover from £445m to £1.2bn.

The fund’s latest accounts revealed that the total pool of profits for its 23 partners rose to a record £940m in the 12 months to March, up from £315m the year prior.

While the results do not disclose the identity of the highest-paid partner, Mr Rokos is widely considered the fund’s top earner, who founded the firm in 2015 and is its majority owner.

Mr Rokos, who is one of Britain’s leading hedge fund managers, was also previously a donor to the Conservatives, giving the party around £2m between 2009 and 2015. He made his last donation in 2018.

He also owns the centuries-old, Grade I listed Tottenham House, one of England’s grandest stately homes. Earlier this year, Mr Rokos made headlines by applying for planning permission to install around 1,100 solar panels to provide power to the 100-bed mansion.

Under the plans, two acres of the manor estate – currently used for silage and grazing dairy cows – would be developed to house solar panels. These would provide nearly one third of the power required to run the estate.

Tottenham House was part of the Savernake Estate, once a royal hunting forest with significant links to Henry VIII. The family of Jane Seymour, the King’s third wife, took ownership of the home before passing it down through several generations.

Mr Rokos has been working to restore the house into a family home.

He has launched a series of ambitious construction projects, such as underground tunnels connecting the main house to the stables. He has also installed a tennis pavilion and a cinema.

Before launching Rokos Capital, he also co-founded Brevan Howard, another City hedge fund.

Unlike most large hedge funds, which have multiple portfolio managers making bets on the market, Mr Rokos is the main trader at Rokos Capital.

He is supported by a team of analysts and senior investment professionals, which the firm calls investment officers.

Rokos Capital Management was contacted for comment.

data.title: BP faces grilling over use of Chinese steel at net zero power plant
data.description:
BP will be hauled in front of ministers on Monday to answer questions about why Chinese steel is being used to build a taxpayer-backed net zero power plant on Teesside.

Chris McDonald, the industry minister, will hold emergency talks with the energy giant after it emerged that its Net Zero Teesside project had given a Chinese steelmaker a £5m contract for 7,000 tonnes of green steel.

Mr McDonald said he was deeply disappointed by the decision and that he expected projects funded by the taxpayer to use British-made products as much as possible.

Net Zero Teesside (NZT), a project to build Britain’s first gas plant featuring green carbon capture technology, was this weekend revealed to have given China’s Modern Heavy Industries the contract for part of the steel needed to build the facility.

The company was awarded the deal because it can produce green steel using electric furnaces. Britain currently does not have commercial-scale green steel production.

Mr McDonald said: “We have been clear that we expect Net Zero Teesside Power to use UK steel wherever possible and I will be meeting the company urgently to discuss this.

“The people of the country and the Government share an ambition to grow industry, open factories and create well-paid jobs in communities that need them.

“To do this we need all stakeholders to be pulling in the same direction and that means maximising British-made products, in projects paid for by the British taxpayer.”

“We will continue to champion British steelmaking and our steel communities now and for generations to come.”

Lord Houchen, the Tees Valley mayor, said he would also raise the issue in a meeting with Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, on Monday, and would write to BP over the matter. Industry body UK Steel said the decision “beggars belief”.

NZT, a £4bn project on the site of the former Redcar steelworks on Teesside, is set to be the world’s first gas-powered electricity plant with its own carbon capture and storage system, capable of powering around one million homes from 2028.

It is supported by the taxpayer through an arrangement in which it is guaranteed a minimum income for 15 years, even if electricity prices drop.

Lord Houchen said: “It’s crazy that a multibillion-pound project underwritten by the UK Government, and therefore the UK taxpayer, will be using Chinese steel.”

He said BP had questions to answer. “BP is the project lead and the main sponsor shareholder of the project,” he said.

Gareth Stace, director-general of UK Steel, said “the decision to buy 7,000 tonnes of Chinese steel rather than sourcing it from British Steel just miles away, beggars belief”.

“For a major, taxpayer-supported project, this is deeply disappointing and should never have been allowed to happen,” he added.

“The Government has been clear about its ambition to back British industry, strengthen domestic supply chains and treat steel as a strategic national asset. UK Steel strongly supports that direction of travel, which makes BP’s decision all the harder to understand.”

The contract was previously reported to have been awarded by French business Technip, which is building the carbon capture part of the project.

The Government said in 2024 it would spend some £22bn supporting carbon capture projects, which aim to fight global warming by removing carbon from emissions when generating electricity.

BP owns 75pc of NZT, with Norwegian energy company Equinor owning the remaining 25pc. BP directed questions to NZT.

NZT said Modern had been awarded a steel contract along with the British group William Hare.

“British structural steel engineering group William Hare, alongside Modern Heavy Industries, has been awarded a contract to provide steel structures for NZT Power, following a competitive procurement process,” it said.

“NZT Power’s major contractors remain on track to deliver more than 50pc UK content, with around £1bn of UK-based subcontracts already awarded.”

data.title: BBC strikes deal to make bespoke TV shows for YouTube
data.description:
The BBC has struck a landmark deal to make shows for YouTube as it grapples with an exodus of viewers to the streaming service.

The public service broadcaster will begin making programmes specifically for YouTube under the terms of a deal that could be announced as early as next week, the Financial Times reported.

These programmes, which would primarily be aimed at younger viewers, would subsequently be shown on the corporation’s own streaming platforms iPlayer and Sounds.

The BBC is seeking to face up to the existential threat posed by YouTube, which is increasingly drawing viewers away from traditional TV.

Figures from the official ratings agency Barb this week revealed YouTube has now overtaken the BBC for audience reach for the first time in history.

Almost 52 million people watched YouTube on their televisions, smartphones or laptops in December, compared with the 50.8 million Britons who tuned in to the BBC.

More people also watched YouTube than BBC channels in October and November, signalling the end of the corporation’s dominance of British airwaves for the last 90 years.

Last year, Ofcom urged the BBC and other traditional broadcasters to make programming specifically for YouTube, warning that they risked fading into irrelevance as younger viewers increasingly shunned conventional TV channels.

The corporation has previously used the streaming platform solely for trailers and clips, with audiences only able to view its shows on iPlayer – though senior executives have recently acknowledged the need to change this approach.

The BBC is now expected to develop new formats to be shown on YouTube. This is said to include programming specifically designed to combat the spread of misinformation online.

Alongside shows aimed at younger viewers, the BBC’s YouTube output will also include children’s programming and sports-related content. Some older series may also be shown on the platform, though this is not said to be a focus of the deal.

Advertising revenue

In addition to luring in defecting audiences, the YouTube deal will allow the BBC to generate more advertising revenues from shows viewed outside the UK.

Tim Davie, the BBC’s outgoing director-general, has long emphasised the need to bolster the corporation’s commercial offering to offset a loss of licence fee revenues.

Income from the licence fee has dropped by 30pc in real terms over the last decade owing to government-imposed freezes, while the BBC lost more than £1bn last year from people evading or cancelling the licence fee, which is expected to rise from £174.50 to £181 in April.

It comes at a crucial time for the BBC, which is entering discussions with the Government about the future of the licence fee before the end of the current charter period in 2027.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, has said she will consider a range of potential reforms including replacing the levy with advertising or subscriptions.

The BBC is also fighting a $10bn (£7.5bn) lawsuit filed by Donald Trump over a misleading edit in an episode of Panorama, which was first reported by The Telegraph.

Lawyers for the broadcaster have asked a judge to dismiss the suit, arguing that the Florida court venue is “improper” and that the US president has failed to show the documentary caused him harm.

The partnership is the latest example of traditional broadcasters seeking collaboration and even consolidation with streaming rivals.

Last summer ITV struck a deal with Disney that will see the two companies carry each other’s programming on their respective streaming platforms.

ITV is also locked in discussions about a £1.6bn takeover by Sky owner Comcast, with executives arguing that greater scale is needed to compete with the likes of Netflix and YouTube.

The BBC declined to comment. YouTube has been contacted for comment.

data.title: Trump warned credit card crackdown will trigger financial crisis for millions
data.description:
Donald Trump’s crackdown on credit cards risks pushing millions of poor Americans into financial crisis, some of Wall Street’s most powerful groups have warned.

Mr Trump is pressing ahead with plans to temporarily cap credit card interest rates for 12 months at 10pc from January 20.

However, the proposal is facing a backlash over fears it will force banks to cancel cards and pull back lending to consumers with the lowest credit scores.

On Monday, the American Bankers Association (ABA), which represents major lenders like JP Morgan, hedge fund billionaire Bill Ackman and the Bank Policy Institute (BPI) all rounded on Mr Trump over the plan.

Mr Ackman, who has publicly supported Mr Trump, added: “My concern about capping rates at 10pc is that doing so will inevitably cause millions of Americans to have their cards cancelled as credit card companies lose the ability to adequately price subprime credit risk.

“Consumers denied credit cards will be forced to turn to loan sharks whose rates and terms will be vastly worse for borrowers.”

The BPI said the cap would “be devastating for millions of American families and small business owners … the very consumers this proposal intends to help”. The ABA said the move would push consumers towards “less regulated, more costly alternatives”.

Mr Trump’s announcement sent shares in US credit card providers sliding on Monday, with Capital One leading the fallers plunging by as much as 6.24pc.

In the UK, as much as £8bn was wiped off the market value of British banks. Shares in Barclays, which has a US credit card business, fell by 3.3pc in London. Shares in NatWest were also down by 1.2pc, even though the bank does not operate a US credit card business.

The plan has stoked widespread fears because credit cards are so vital for the US economy, with the number of accounts hitting a record 642 million in the third quarter of 2025.

This means the US has nearly twice as many credit card accounts as it does people. Total credit card debt is at a record $1.23tn (£891bn) – up by 39pc since the pandemic began.

In theory, capping at 10pc should dramatically reduce borrowers’ costs because it would more than halve the average rate paid, which was 22.5pc in November.

At no point since at least 1994, when Federal Reserve data began, has the rate been as low as 10pc. According to Vanderbilt University, a 10pc cap would save US consumers $73bn net a year.

However, analysts warn it is precisely the people who Trump is trying to help that will be hit the hardest by his rate cap because it will force banks to do less lending.

Justin Begley, economist at Moody’s Analytics said: “If the government does indeed enforce this policy on the banks, then it’s just going to become completely unprofitable to lend to people that are of higher credit risk.

“I would disagree with people who are saying that the banks can absorb this. The banks have to be able to properly price credit risk.”

Banks may simply cancel subprime borrowers’ credit cards outright, Mr Begley added. “That’s something I have heard banks talking about doing. They’re going to want to protect themselves.”

Jefferies economist Mohit Kumar said: “It is likely that lower rates will push credit providers to reassess their lending criteria and the policy ends up creating more harm than good.”

Subprime borrowers have a credit card default rate of 1.2pc, double the overall default rate, according to Moody’s. Currently, subprime borrowers make up 21pc of accounts in default. During the financial crisis, the rate was closer to half.

The measure will hit at a time when low and middle income US households are becoming increasingly overwhelmed with their debts.

The share of Americans who are falling seriously behind on their credit card payments has already soared to a near 15-year high.

Debt advice firms have been warning that America’s cost of living crisis is increasingly hitting middle class households, as families turn to credit just to cover the costs of basic necessities.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Burnham’s skyscraper tycoon takes £40m dividend after taxpayer loan
data.description:
A skyscraper tycoon backed by Andy Burnham took £40m in dividends from a taxpayer-backed project in Manchester, The Telegraph can reveal.

Daren Whitaker moved the money over a period of three years from WB Developments, a company backed by Greater Manchester Combined Authority (GMCA).

It was received by another company owned by Mr Whitaker.

The disclosures, contained in Companies House filings, will heighten scrutiny of the relationship between the GMCA and Mr Whitaker, who has received more than £700m in taxpayer loans to build luxury skyscrapers in Manchester.

Under a loan agreement seen by The Telegraph, Mr Whitaker’s WB Developments was barred from paying dividends “except with the prior written consent of the lender”.

The dividends were awarded in instalments between 2017 and 2019 from WB Developments, which received £42.5m in cash from the GMCA to help build Wilburn Basin, a 491-bedroom apartment block in Salford.

The first was a £17.7m dividend in 2017, followed by £15.4m and £6.8m in payments in 2018 and 2019.

Mr Whitaker is the ultimate owner of WB Developments, according to filings on Companies House, which falls within his Renaker empire.

The payments were made despite restrictions on dividend payments contained in the initial £42.5m loan agreement between the GMCA, which is chaired by the Mayor of Greater Manchester, and Mr Whitaker for Wilburn Basin. It was struck in 2016 before Mr Burnham was elected Mayor of Greater Manchester.

Asked about the dividends last night, the GMCA refused to say whether officials were aware of the payments or if they granted approval.

The loan was to be repaid in July 2018, two years after construction started on Wilburn Basin.

A spokesman for the GMCA said: “The loan in question was repaid in full, with interest, eight years ago. It was repaid nine months before the deadline, bringing significant benefits to the taxpayer.”

The loans from the GMCA to Mr Whitaker have prompted a High Court claim from rival developer Aubrey Weis. He has alleged Renaker was able to cash in on Manchester’s building boom by unfairly accessing public funds.

Mr Weis has argued that the loans were effectively subsidies for Renaker, which used the cash to build tower blocks that contained no affordable housing. Renaker has not commented and the GMCA has consistently denied the allegations.

A ruling handed down by the Competition Appeal Tribunal last year rejected Mr Weis’s claims.

He is appealing against the decision.

Mr Burnham has sought to defend the loans, telling the BBC last month that the GMCA supported Renaker because “London-based lenders wouldn’t give us the money”.

“They had this downer on Manchester,” he said.

The Telegraph revealed late last year that Mr Whitaker had listed his main residence as Monaco on Companies House. However, this was later changed back to Britain.

When asked about Mr Whitaker’s residency last month, Mr Burnham denied knowledge of his circumstances but said: “I can’t comment on people’s decisions about their lives. People have got to be free to where they live.

“It’s not the case that someone has walked away with anything other than having invested in our city and built it out. The towers are helping us to bring investors to the city.”

After building a string of different skyscrapers, Renaker is now pursuing its most ambitious project to date.

This is called the Lighthouse, a skyscraper consisting of 642 high-end flats with a restaurant on its top floor.

Renaker was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Give under-22s free bus travel, says Green Party
data.description:
The Green Party is calling for free bus travel in England for those under the age of 22.

Zack Polanski, the party’s leader, said introducing the universal bus pass for the group could help young people shut out of employment and training by the cost of living.

The party said the scheme would be self-financing, arguing that increased bus usage would boost the economy.

Mr Polanski said: “Young people are increasingly shut out of employment and training by the cost of living crisis, which often hits the youngest the hardest.

“This Labour Government has made a dire situation for young people even worse with the poorest getting even poorer and rich getting even richer.

“Free bus travel for the young is one part of improving the lives of younger people and showing what a richer and greener country looks like if we invest in people and not hand over our assets to the super-rich and corporations.”

The scheme would bring England into line with Scotland, where those aged five to 22 have been entitled to free bus travel since 2022.

Under-18s in London already travel for free on buses with other discounts available for some up to the age of 25, while some bus companies across England also offer discounts for young people.

Mr Polanski said Green Party councillors, if elected in May’s local polls, will seek to bring in the universal bus scheme in their areas.

He said: “Green councillors elected in May will be using their influence to implement this scheme in their areas and we will be applying political pressure at a national level for a scheme recommended by the transport select committee last year to be made real.

“Free bus travel for the young is part of our mission to make hope normal again.”

The party pointed to research by KPMG estimating that 2.2 million commuters rely on buses to get to work and generate £72bn in collective income each year.

Anyone at state pension age in Britain can currently claim a national older person’s bus pass.

In London those over 60 can get an Oyster card with free travel around the capital.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Elon Musk has parked his tanks on BT’s lawn
data.description:
As the repressive regime in Iran closed down the internet, it came to the rescue. It has become a vital tool for Ukrainians in their battle against the Russians. And more mundanely, it allows you to send a couple of WhatsApps while you are flying across the Atlantic, if you’re on the right airline.

We typically think of Elon Musk’s satellite-based broadband provider Starlink as only being useful in a handful of limited circumstances. But that is starting to change. Musk is about to park his formidable tanks on the lawns of BT and the rest of Britain’s internet companies – and not before time, the whole market could easily be blown apart.

After a dreadful few years, 2025 was a lot better for Britain’s largest telecoms business, BT. The shares rose by almost 30pc over the last year, and the threat of a takeover attempt receded as its performance improved. Its huge capital spend on upgrading its broadband network is nearing completion, allowing it to generate lots of easy profits, while smaller rivals were starting to go under. Only last week, the “vulture fund” FitzWalter Capital applied to put G Network, which provides high-speed broadband in London, into administration.

Allison Kirkby, BT’s chief executive, is probably hoping for a decent year. But broadband incumbents like BT are now facing a new threat that none of them saw coming: Musk.

Sure, he has plenty of critics, and the Government led by Sir Keir Starmer is shamefully trying to hustle Musk out of the country. But there is no denying that Musk is the greatest entrepreneur of his generation. As well as building Tesla into a major force in cars and transforming the social media site X, he has turned SpaceX and its Starlink unit into a company now worth $800bn (£600bn).

One reason why investors are putting such a huge value on the company is becoming clear. It is becoming a viable domestic broadband provider. In the UK, Starlink has just started offering high-speed internet for as little as £35 a month, down from £55, in certain areas. That is cheaper than BT’s equivalent £40 service and the £36-a-month Virgin Media offer. Even once the connection fee is taken into account, it still works out cheaper than BT over a two-year contract. It is starting to become a player.

The mainstream view in the telecoms industry is that satellite broadband will never be a mass market proposition. “In fixed broadband, satellite costs are still a multiple of the average costs of traditional networks, only becoming competitive for a proportion of the hardest 1pc of households to reach,” argued a note from Enders Analysis. Well, perhaps. For now, it is certainly true that providing broadband access via satellite is much more expensive than via a fibre-optic cable running along your street. It will struggle to attract more than a handful of customers living in very remote areas.

But that is not necessarily the whole story. Musk has deep, deep pockets. SpaceX is already worth more than 40 times BT’s entire market value, and once it completes an expected Initial Public Offering later this year, Musk is likely to become the world’s first trillionaire. He has the money, and we know from his record that he doesn’t mind spending it if he needs to build a business. Nor does he care very much about what his shareholders think. If he has to make some losses for a few years to build a position in the British, or indeed the wider European broadband industry, he will be perfectly willing to do so. It will pay off soon enough, and even if it doesn’t, the losses will hardly break him.

SpaceX can raise vast sums of fresh capital whenever it wants, at virtually zero cost. If he needs money to expand, it will be available. That certainly is not true of BT or any of the other incumbents. One point is surely clear. Musk is the most restlessly hyper-competitive tycoon on the planet, and he builds businesses mainly for the satisfaction of it. If he sees an opportunity, he will seize it and bulldoze aside any obstacles.

For consumers, of course, that is very welcome. The broadband market has been characterised by high prices and often very poor service. An analysis by Uswitch, published only this week, found that millions of households face an 11pc rise in broadband prices this year, or more than three times the rate of inflation, as fixed-price deals come to an end. Meanwhile, anyone who has been stuck on the phone for hours talking to what is optimistically called a “helpline” – probably an AI chatbot – won’t need much persuading to switch to a cheaper offering, especially if it has a faster or more reliable service.

To put this politely, broadband is not a business with a great deal of brand loyalty. All the major players have focused on cutting costs and ramping up profits rather than keeping their customers happy. That works fine so long as there isn’t much competition. When a rival appears, however, it can quickly prove very costly.

Right now, the major broadband players view Starlink as a peripheral provider serving a small niche market. So, probably, do most customers. But the auto giants thought that about Tesla, and we all know how that worked out for them. In reality, Musk can build a whole new market in less time than it takes traditional businesses such as BT to organise a “strategy awayday”. They underestimate him at their peril. Starlink is parking its tank, or rather its satellites or their lawns. BT needs to come up with a response and fast – no one should bet against Musk blowing the competition away.

data.title: Europe’s beer giant in trouble as thirst for lager fades
data.description:
Heineken boss Dolf van den Brink was in ebullient mood last year when unveiled a new lager to take the Dutch brewer “into territory where beer brands couldn’t go”.

Heineken 0.0 Ultra, a “beer” with zero alcohol, zero calories and zero sugar, would target “the next generation” of drinkers by reaping the benefits of Gen Z cutting back on excess.

It was a sign of Van den Brink’s hubristic faith in non-alcoholic beers – but also of the desperate scramble by drinks bosses to solve a crisis engulfing the drinks industry.

Just a month after his comments, Heineken stunned investors with a shock profit warning after a slump in beer sales. Van den Brink warned worse would come in 2026, sending shares cratering.

Now, the man who promised to pilot Heineken into the future, is gone. The 52-year-old announced his departure last week after six years in charge. One analyst said he had “not delivered” on the company’s high expectations.

His exit is a sign of the treacherous waters drinks giants must sail as demand for lager wanes.

In Britain, the company is taking drastic measures with dizzying price rises and watered-down beers aimed at boosting its bottom line.

Heineken is responsible for 30pc of lager pint sales in British pubs, owning brands like Fosters, Amstel, Birra Moretti, John Smiths, Sagres, Tiger as well as the Heineken range.

Despite some recent successes such as Cruzcampo and Murphy’s Irish Stout, the brewer is planning to raise prices on drinks supplied to pubs by 2.7pc next month because of the worrying sales figures.

It follows years of major price rises as the company struggles to keep hold of consumers who go out less often and drink less when they do. Keg prices were upped by 2.5pc last year and 1.7pc in 2024.

Heineken said it made the decision to raise prices in the face of soaring costs for the brewing and hospitality sector, not least surging beer duty.

At the same time, pub-goers will soon find themselves paying more for less, as Heineken slashes its beers’ alcohol by volume (ABV) level.

Foster’s, which had already been cut from 4pc to 3.7pc in 2023, is set to be further reduced to 3.4pc next month. John Smith’s Extra Smooth – the company’s top-selling ale – was similarly reduced from 3.6pc to 3.4pc last year.

Heineken is far from the only brewer weakening its beers. Asahi, Coors and Carlsberg have also cut the strength of certain drinks in the last three years to land in lower tax bands.

Yet consumers will only tolerate lowered alcohol levels up to a point, says Julie Palmer, of business insolvency firm Begbies Traynor.

“I think around three per cent is quite a critical level,” she said. “Once you get below that, you start thinking, ‘Am I drinking alcohol at all?’”

“I think where this probably works better is with things like sports organisations, such as football, which carry brands like Fosters or Heineken. People are drinking volume and not really thinking about ABV.”

Cutting the ABV also has another benefit: potentially massive cost savings for Heineken and others.

Under the reformed UK alcohol duty system introduced in August 2023, beer below 3.5pc is taxed at a lower duty rate, creating a clear threshold that many brewers actively aim for.

Alcohol duty is set to rise by an inflation-busting 3.66pc at the start of February, equating to around 2p on the price of a pint in a pub.

This duty is charged per litre of pure alcohol, so dropping a beer from 4pc to just under 3.5pc both reduces the pure alcohol content and moves it into a lower tax band, saving brewers around 8p to 12p per pint in duty, with much larger savings at scale.

Cutting ABV very slightly can be an easy win for brewers, since it is a change consumers tend not to notice, says Marten Lodewijks, from IWSR.

“They know at a basic level that spirits have more alcohol than wine, but beyond that, they are not very good at knowing alcohol content,” he explains. “Unless they are directly comparing ABV on a can, by and large, it goes unnoticed.”

Incentivising brewers to churn out weaker beer also suits a Government keen to reduce the public’s overall alcohol intake. “If the Government drives the regime by giving tax benefits for going lower, then companies will test the market,” says Palmer.

However, the Campaign for Real Ale (Camra) has criticised the move.

“What Heineken and other global giants have done is dilute their recipes to hit the lower tax band, without reducing prices, and sometimes hiking them. This is something that independent brewers can’t afford to do or won’t do because it will compromise quality,” said Camra’s Tim Webb.

Changing consumer tastes are also driving demand for weaker drinks.

Brewers that rely on shifting millions of litres of beer a day, like Heineken, have been forced to adapt to pubgoers drinking less often and less heavily.

Britain is becoming more sober, with the typical adult consuming 10 drinks per week on average last year down from 14 per week in 2003, according IWSR.

Heineken originally launched its first 0.0 offering in 2016, with Van den Brink saying it was an attempt to break down the “taste barrier” left by poor offerings such as Kaliber, Buckler and Clausthaler.

Yet its 0.0 offering was noted to have underperformed, beaten to the punch by brands like Lucky Saint.

“If anything, the trend is moving towards drinks that are simply really nice,” says Palmer “If it is a hot, sunny day, you might not mind if it has a lower alcohol content.”

Lodewijks says: “The growth of these products is solving a need for moderation. You might want to lower your alcohol intake but still be part of the occasion, and soft drinks do not always lend themselves to that.”

Behaviour among drinkers is also diverging, especially at home. Households are turning away from regular-strength beer at a faster rate than lower-strength ones.

With brewers edging towards the floor of what alcohol level consumers will tolerate, and pubgoers increasingly opting for spirits, wine, or alcohol-free drinks – the days of “peak lager” are long gone, says Lodewijks.

“In developed markets, beer as a whole has been trending down, and that impact is felt most strongly in the core lager segment, particularly mid-priced standard lager,” he adds.

British drinkers have moved on to so-called premium brands, for which they are willing to pay more. These include lagers like Peroni, Camden Hells and Leffe.

Craft beers, now widely available, have also taken the shine off bog standard fizzy lagers.

In one of his final appearances as Heineken chief, van den Brink said the brewer had been “the pioneer and the leader” for the non-alcohol movement.

Drinkers taking a sip of their weaker pints next month may reluctantly be forced to agree.

Heineken was approached for comment.

data.title: Reeves ploughs tens of millions of pounds into self-driving car company
data.description:
Rachel Reeves is preparing to inject tens of millions of pounds into an Oxford self-driving car pioneer as Labour gets ready to unleash autonomous vehicles on Britain’s roads.

The £28bn National Wealth Fund (NWF), which is backed by the Treasury, is nearing a deal to plough taxpayer cash into Oxa, the driverless car start-up founded by experts from Oxford University.

Oxa, formerly Oxbotica, was the first company to trial driverless cars on UK roads in 2016 and has previously raised more than £180m from investors.

It has focused on developing software that can make any vehicle autonomous, rather than building self-driving cars itself. Its technology is used to power driverless shuttle buses and industrial vehicles.

The Telegraph revealed in December that Oxa had secured about £15m from existing investors, including the venture capital arm of BP, and that it was in talks for a further “landmark frontier AI investment” early this year.

In September, Jensen Huang, the boss of Nvidia, also expressed an interest in backing Oxa.

At the time, he called the company an “incredible robotics, autonomous driving company” and said he would “invest in your next round”.

A deal with Oxa, which was founded by Oxford University professor Paul Newman in 2014, would represent one of the government’s biggest direct bets on an AI business.

It comes as the Government prepares to launch driverless taxi and bus trials on British roads later this year.

Ride-hailing businesses Uber and Lyft have announced plans to test self-driving taxis under new rules in the UK. Tesla has also been piloting its Full Self-Driving technology in Britain.

Wayve, another UK driverless technology business that has raised more than £1bn, has a deal with Uber to launch public self-driving taxi trials in the UK later this year.

The new funding comes via the NWF, which was launched by Labour in 2024 as a successor to the UK Infrastructure Bank. The fund is operationally independent from the Treasury, but was set up to fuel the government’s growth agenda with key investments.

Its typical direct investment size is £25m to £50m. Its cash injections are intended to help incentivise hundreds of millions in further funding from private investors. The fund has so far made more than £200m in equity investments.

It has also provided billions of pounds in debt funding to clean power and battery manufacturing projects. In its first year under Labour, the fund invested £3.6bn.

State backing would represent a major boost for Oxa, which has grappled with mounting losses and saw its valuation slashed by a key investor last year.

IP Group, an early investor in the business, disclosed that it had cut Oxa’s value by two-thirds to £120m, down from more than £350m previously.

An Oxa spokesman and the NWF declined to comment. The Treasury was contacted for comment.

data.title: South East Water investors fail to publicly back boss after supply crisis
data.description:
The embattled boss of South East Water is under fresh pressure to resign after two shareholders refused to publicly back him.

The NatWest Group Pension Fund, the bank’s £35bn retirement scheme, and Desjardins, the Canadian financial giant, which each own a 25pc stake in the water supplier, failed to express their support for chief executive Dave Hinton when approached by The Telegraph.

Instead, Desjardins called for supply issues to be “fully resolved with urgency” after the company left tens of thousands of customers in Tunbridge Wells and the surrounding area without water.

In a statement, Desjardins urged South East Water to “address the resilience of their services” following the second major failure in the space of just a few weeks.

It comes after Mike Martin, MP for Tunbridge Wells, urged South East Water’s investors to oust those at the top of the crisis-stricken water supplier.

“I am now appealing directly to South East Water’s owners to sack its leadership and bring in a turnaround team who can restore our water supply,” he told local newspaper Kent Online.

Nearly 30,000 properties have been left without water in Tunbridge Wells and its surrounding areas this month, just weeks after taps ran dry for thousands of homes before Christmas.

Desjardins is the latest major backer to address the performance issues at South East Water.

The NatWest pension fund was the first to break cover, saying it was “extremely concerned by the impact these incidents are having”.

Shortly afterwards, infrastructure fund Utilities Trust of Australia, which owns the remaining 50pc of South-East, issued a separate statement, describing the failures as “deeply concerning”.

The reproach adds to a storm of criticism directed at South East Water and its chief executive, who remains in post despite growing calls for him to stand down.

Ofwat has opened an investigation into the company after Sir Keir Starmer, the Prime Minister, called the situation “totally unacceptable”.

The latest shortages emerged last week after Mr Hinton was subjected to an intense grilling by senior MPs over his handling of the affair.

Over the past two months, South East has blamed cold weather, power supply failures and bad chemicals at its treatment plant. Mr Hinton told MPs his performance was worthy of an “eight out of 10” mark.

Environment Secretary Emma Reynolds said she had met Chris Train on Thursday and told him to “get a grip” of the situation. 

However, Mr Train told Kent Online: “We fully support the executive team.”

If Ofwat finds South East Water has not provided high standards of customer service, then the firm could be stripped of its licence. It could also be fined 10pc of its annual turnover.

Desjardins said it wanted to see “the swift restoration of full service to all affected households and businesses that rely on South East Water for supply”. 

It added: “As a shareholder, we are in close contact with the board.”

South East Water states that it supplies “top quality drinking” to its 2.3 million customers.

A South East Water spokesman said: “As outlined in the response from Desjardins, our focus is entirely on restoring and stabilising supplies to all affected customers as soon as possible.”
