data.title: Miliband net zero plans hit by surging reliance on gas
data.description:
Britain’s reliance on fossil fuels has increased for the first time in four years, dealing a major blow to Ed Miliband’s hopes of decarbonising the grid by 2030.

Gas-fired power plants generated 26.8pc of power in 2025, a rise of 1.1pc from the year prior, according to new figures from the Government’s National Energy System Operator (Neso).

This stemmed largely from reduced nuclear output across Britain, as the country’s nine remaining reactors were hit by technical faults and unplanned blackouts.

The latest figures also revealed that the UK relied on imports for up to 14pc of its electricity last year, with London and the South East frequently powered by energy from France.

Increased reliance on fossil fuels and continued imports from overseas will raise fresh scrutiny over the Energy Secretary’s goal of attaining clean power by 2030.

To achieve this, Mr Miliband has vowed to spend billions of pounds on intermittent renewables such as wind and solar farms, with the cost of this expansion set to be passed through to households and businesses.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, has accused Mr Miliband of needlessly driving up bills and putting the country’s energy security at risk.

She said: “What this data shows is that we need reliable, 24/7 power for when the wind doesn’t blow and the sun doesn’t shine – and that means more nuclear and more gas.

“Ed Miliband’s plan to build more wind and solar farms than ever before will do nothing to power our economy on a cold, windless, cloudy day.

“His ideological approach to energy policy means British families are going to continue to see their bills rise, jobs lost overseas, and the risk of blackouts increase.”

The Energy Department hit back by blaming the previous government for “years of dither and delay on new nuclear”, with a spokesman claiming that this left the country reliant on power plants dating back to the 1960s.

Total nuclear output fell from 13.7pc of UK generation in 2024 to 11.8pc last year, the latest Neso figures show, with a string of plant failures to blame.

This month, the Hartlepool nuclear power station was forced to shut down both its reactors for unplanned repairs, while Heysham 2 and Torness have also scaled back output.

EDF, the French power firm that operates the UK’s remaining reactors, has already closed several, while the rest have been kept in operation beyond their original lifespans.

However, all but Sizewell B will have to close by March 2030, making it crucial for Hinckley Point C, the proposed nuclear power station in Somerset, to be ready by that point.

Meanwhile, the latest data reveal a steady rise in low-carbon output in recent years, with wind and solar contributing 36.5pc to total power generation in 2025, up from 24pc in 2019.

The planned expansion of green energy under Labour means this figure is set to rise further, with plans for hundreds of new wind, solar and battery plants across England.

The revelations come just a week before Mr Miliband is due to announce the latest round of renewables contracts, under which developers will get guaranteed minimum prices for the power they generate.

The cost of those subsidies will be added to energy bills, with wind and solar poised to be the biggest beneficiaries.

However, some analysts warn that the expansion of renewables may not be enough to help decarbonise the grid in time.

Adam Berman, of Energy UK, which represents the supply industry, said Mr Miliband’s target of decarbonising the grid by 2030 should be seen as an aspiration rather than being set in stone.

“There may be a blip upwards now but the long-term trend is clear,” he said.

“Gas-fired power generation produced about 39pc of electricity back 2019 and now its down to 25pc. The Clean Power 2030 target really has focused minds in government in a way that was not the case before.”

A spokesman for the Energy Department said: “These [Neso] statistics reflect decisions made by previous governments, including years of dither and delay on new nuclear.

“This Government is turning this around, already consenting record amounts of solar – enough for the equivalent of 8.5 million homes. These projects take time to build, but we are making the decisions to benefit Britain for years to come.”

Ana Musat, of Renewable UK, the wind industry’s trade body, said: “The UK’s wind and solar farms generated a record amount of clean power last year, but as our nuclear fleet is retiring we’ve been forced to burn more gas, making it even more important to speed up the delivery of more clean power.

“We expect new generation records to be set this year with a huge pipeline of renewable energy projects under construction or being planned. We’re working closely with the Government to ensure that achieve clean power by 2030, and next week we’re hoping to secure a record amount of new offshore wind capacity in the next auction for projects to go ahead.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain risks missing out on gas price ‘lifeline’
data.description:
Britain risks missing out on an economic boost from collapsing gas prices as green levies keep energy costs high, a new report has warned.

A global glut of natural gas will see wholesale prices fall sharply across the UK and Europe, potentially almost halving by the end of the decade, according to leading energy consultancy Wood Mackenzie.

Analysts said falling prices offered “a lifeline” to UK and European heavy industry and “could provide material support”.

However, Britain and Europe risk missing out on the benefit because surging carbon taxes will keep energy costs high. The two main taxes on burning gas for power currently add about £25 per megawatt hour (MWh) to costs in the UK, and the levy is designed to ratchet upwards, potentially doubling by 2030.

Massimo Di Odoardo, vice president of gas research at Wood Mackenzie, said: “UK and European prices are set to almost halve compared to levels in 2025.

“However, lower wholesale gas prices will not fully translate into lower energy bills for industrial users across the EU and UK. Taxes and levies and the need to pay higher prices for carbon emissions will limit some of the reduction.”

The prediction will add to scrutiny of Labour’s net zero plan, which involves racing to eliminate gas from the energy system by 2030. Critics say the artificial deadline is creating high costs for homes and businesses.

Labour is also preparing to link Britain’s carbon taxes to the EU’s, a move that has already led to a jump in costs for industry.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, has argued that transitioning the UK to green energy will boost energy security and lower bills, but industry leaders warn that even if that happens, it will be too late.

Sir Jim Ratcliffe, chairman of the chemicals conglomerate Ineos, has warned that carbon taxes on oil and gas have left the UK unable to compete with rivals. Ineos shut down its Grangemouth Refinery last year and has warned that its neighbouring chemical works may follow.

The cost of gas and the associated carbon taxes have become a crucial issue for heavy industry. The output of industrial firms such as paper mills, steelmakers, glassblowers and pottery fell by a third between 2021 and 2024 due to high energy prices, according to a recent analysis by the Office for National Statistics.

Wood Mackenzie’s report warned: “The single most important obstacle to increasing European industrial competitiveness going forward is its ambitious decarbonisation agenda.

“Even as natural gas prices fall, industries could face higher overall energy bills.”

Wood Mackenzie’s gas price predictions are based on expectations that global supplies of liquefied natural gas (LNG) will grow faster than demand.

“LNG suppliers, particularly in the US and Qatar, have responded to high gas prices by investing massively in new supply,” its analysts wrote. “Consequently, traded gas prices in western Europe are set to tumble in the coming years as a new wave of LNG supply hits the market.”

Andreas Schröder, head of gas analytics at energy specialists ICIS, backed Wood Mackenzie’s findings. He said recent investment was “likely to result in periods of oversupply, which is broadly positive for European industry as it puts downward pressure on gas prices”.

American gas prices are currently far lower than Europe’s, but are expected to rise because export terminals being built along the US East Coast to export LNG will effectively turn the USA and Europe into a single market, equalising prices. It means US gas prices, which are now less than half those in the UK, are likely to surge 40pc by 2030.

A Government spokesman said its aim remained to cut the UK’s reliance on imported gas.

They said: “We are getting off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel markets controlled by petrostates and dictators, and with our record-breaking renewables auction round, we are taking back control of our own energy. The new renewables we are procuring are 40pc cheaper than new building and operating new gas – and will drive down wholesale prices.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Drug-driving causes more road deaths than alcohol
data.description:
Drugs are now a bigger factor in road deaths than alcohol, official figures show.

The number of deceased drivers who tested positive for drugs increased by 78 per cent – from 106 to 189 – in the decade to 2023, according to the Department for Transport (DfT) and police data.

By contrast, the number of dead motorists with alcohol proven to be present in their system rose by five per cent in the same period, from 162 to 171.

Sir Keir Starmer now stands accused of ignoring the bigger problem of drug-driving while planning to reduce the drink-driving limit. The Government’s proposals have prompted a backlash from MPs and publicans, who say they will put pubs under more pressure following an increase in business rates.

Britain lost an average of one pub each day in 2025, and industry bosses have warned that rising tax bills and wages, on top of higher energy costs, will drive hundreds more out of business.

The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save the UK’s pubs, calling on Labour to stop its assault on locals and cut tax and red tape.

Ministers are now expected to reverse their decision to impose higher costs on Britain’s struggling pubs, saying they are working on relief measures to be announced in the coming days. But the about-turn relates to jumps in business rates for landlords, not the new drink-drive limit.

DfT figures show that the percentage of fatal collisions in which drink-driving was involved has been relatively stable over the past 10 years, at 13 per cent.

However, the proportion in which drug-driving played a role doubled from five per cent in 2014 to 10 per cent in 2023.

While drug-driving convictions rose by 13.5 per cent to 27,000 in 2024, the number of drivers convicted of drink-driving offences fell by six per cent to 36,415.

Meanwhile, injuries from drink-driving incidents have significantly decreased since 1980, from around 20,000 annually to about 5,000 since 2020.

Despite this, the Government’s new road strategy proposes “taking tougher action on drink-driving” by reducing the legal limit of 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg, or around a pint.

It would be the most significant reform to road safety laws since 1967, when the blood alcohol limit was first introduced.

Chris Philp, the shadow home secretary, said: “Labour are now proposing even more measures that will endanger country pubs.

“At the same time, the Government is completely failing to do more to address a more rapidly growing road safety issue – drug-driving. More drivers killed in a collision had drugs in their system than alcohol.

“The Government should prioritise toughening up on drug-drivers above measures which will strangle struggling country pubs.”

The British Beer and Pub Association warned that any toughening of measures on drink-driving would harm rural pubs in areas without public transport or reliable taxi services.

A spokesman said: “The pub sector continues to face huge challenges, so any additional policy measures that further impact trade will be of real concern to licensees, especially those in rural areas.”

‘Geographical lottery’

Drug-drivers face similar penalties as those caught drinking, including a minimum 12-month driving ban and up to six months in prison for serious or repeat offences.

Limits for illegal drugs such as cannabis, cocaine, ketamine and heroin are set at extremely low levels, but not at zero in order to account for accidental exposure.

However, Government-funded research has suggested that dangerous drug-drivers have been escaping prosecution – and putting lives at risk – because some police forces ration the number of testing kits issued to officers to just one a day.

The study, by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety, found there was a “geographical lottery” where the best-performing forces were catching 10 times more drug-drivers per head of population than the worst.

Drivers can also escape justice because of delays of four to five months in processing blood tests. Officers have only six months to prosecute.

Penalties to be reviewed

The Government’s new road safety strategy proposes that there should be a review of penalties and mandatory training for drink and drug-driving offences.

It has also pledged to explore alternative processing and evidence collection for drug-driving to “improve speed of results, supporting more robust enforcement outcomes.”

A DfT spokesman said the strategy would “save thousands of lives by targeting the root causes of deaths, including the impact of both alcohol and drugs”.

They added: “We’re determined to crack down on drug-driving, and the strategy includes new measures to modernise how we tackle it, including new testing methods, and powers to suspend driving licenses for those caught under the influence.

“We do not expect the new limit to harm pubs – experience in Scotland shows such changes have minimal impact on local businesses while making roads safer.”

Government sources also denied that the strategy failed to focus on tackling drug-driving, pointing to plans for roadside tests to catch drug-drivers to be stepped up.

Motorists suspected of driving under the influence of drugs would also have their licences suspended immediately under the plans. Roadside tests to catch drug-drivers will also be stepped up.

Police can already use roadside tests as a preliminary check, but results must be confirmed by requiring the driver to take a blood test in a lab. The strategy will set out plans to allow the police to charge suspects based solely on a roadside test.

However, ministers accept that this power will only be used when more reliable tests, that can assess exact amounts of drugs in people’s blood, are available. They also want to encourage police to use roadside tests more widely.

data.title: Asda to axe managers after Christmas disaster
data.description:
Asda has put more than 150 jobs at risk of redundancy after trading collapsed over Christmas, plunging its share of Britain’s grocery market to a new low. 

The troubled supermarket is preparing to axe more than 80 managers and dozens of warehouse staff in its latest attempt to cut costs.

It signals growing pressure on the retail giant, which has so far failed to reverse its fortunes and continues to lose ground to rivals such as Tesco.

Redundancy negotiations will now take place to finalise how many employees will leave Asda, in a move that is likely to dampen morale further across the business.

The cuts are expected to take place across a number of its major distribution centres, with plans to reduce the number of staff dealing with deliveries across its larger warehouses.

Asda said logistics staff in Yorkshire had been placed at risk under plans to outsource some parcel operations to delivery giant Evri.

It would come as the latest in a series of job cuts across the business over the past 18 months, spearheaded by Allan Leighton, the chairman.

A large group of in-store managers were made redundant in July as part of attempts to trim middle management, while hundreds of IT workers were also axed last year following a botched IT upgrade.

Mr Leighton has cut costs as he struggles to rebuild Asda’s market share, which fell to 11.4pc in the 12 weeks to Dec 28.

This came after the grocer’s sales fell by 4.2pc during the period, making Asda the only major supermarket to suffer a drop in sales over Christmas.

By contrast, rivals Tesco and Sainsbury’s won over more customers during the festive period, extending their lead over Asda, Britain’s third-largest supermarket.

Despite his experience overseeing a revival of Asda during the 1990s, Mr Leighton has yet to improve the supermarket’s performance during his second stint at the business.

Its decline has been ongoing ever since private equity giant TDR Capital teamed up with Mohsin and Zuber Issa to buy the business in 2021.

At the time, Asda’s market share stood 14.8pc.

Asda’s turmoil is also reflected in its debt, as traders have sold off bonds in the business after a disappointing Christmas trading period.

A €1.3bn (£1.1bn) term loan issued by Asda’s parent company, Bellis Finco, in 2024 has now fallen to an all-time low of 88 cents on the euro, down from almost 100 early last year.

Pressures have also intensified after leading ratings agency Fitch downgraded the business deeper into junk status last year, threatening the supermarket with higher borrowing costs.

This was on the basis that more investment will be required to lower prices to win back shoppers, which is likely to squeeze profits further.

Asda said there were two separate consultations under way that would help improve efficiency, with neither expected to result in the closure of depots.

“We are proposing to introduce a new regional structure for our transport teams to simplify our distribution network and our deliveries to stores. This involves creating eight regional hubs with a regional transport office coordinating activity across each region,” a spokesman said.

“This approach would deliver several practical benefits, including reducing duplicated tasks, improving regional flexibility, creating more consistent ways of working, and lowering our reliance on agency and haulier support.”

The spokesman said another consultation related to speeding up how it could manage online orders was also running.

He said Asda handled 28 million parcels a year through a network “that was never designed for this level of demand”.

“As a result, fewer than half of our stores can offer next-day parcel collection – a service that customers now expect as standard from all major retailers.

“By partnering with Evri, all 1,200 Asda locations will be able to offer next-day collection and returns, providing customers with a quicker and more convenient service.”

data.title: Amol Rajan quits BBC’s Today programme
data.description:
Amol Rajan has announced he is leaving BBC Radio 4’s Today programme.

The presenter, who joined Today in 2021, is to leave BBC News this summer but will continue to host the broadcaster’s University Challenge.

He will also step back from his Amol Rajan Interviews show, but will continue to present his podcast Radical.

His replacement will announced in due course, the corporation said.

In a statement, Rajan said: “The pips have sounded, and it’s time to get my coat.

“Leaving Today might be mad. This team is the best of the best: consistently world-class, and exceptionally well-led by Owenna Griffiths and Mohit Bakaya. I will always feel like I played in the editorial version of Alex Ferguson’s Manchester United, or Steve Waugh’s Australia.

“I genuinely couldn’t be prouder of Amol Rajan Interviews, which will stand the test of time. Travelling all around the world (or indeed Essex) to spend so much time with so many icons has been an unforgettable privilege.”

He added: “Twenty years after I first flirted with the idea, I’m extremely excited to jump into the Great Digital Narnia of the Creator Economy and build my own company. I’ll still do Britain’s oldest TV quiz and most exciting podcast.

“But Del Boy was my hero growing up, and it’s time to unleash my inner entrepreneur. I am very much not leaving the BBC, Britain’s noblest cultural institution, whose Reithian spirit is such a generous gift to a world in flux. It’s that world I’m heading for, and I’m excited.”

Announcing his departure on social media, Rajan joked to his followers: “The rumours are true, I am indeed the next Director General of the BBC and as it happens I am also batting at number four for England this summer and I am running for Mayor of London, but before all of that I am leaving BBC News.”

Presenter ‘will be sorely missed’

Owenna Griffiths, Today’s editor, called Rajan “an exceptional talent with a rare combination of intellectual dexterity, impressive breadth of interest and a brilliant sense of humour”.

“It has been enormous fun working with him on Today and setting up Radical. He will be sorely missed,” she added.

John McAndrew, the BBC’s director of live and daily news, added: “Amol’s a huge talent who’s been a fantastic addition to the Today line-up. I’d like to thank him for everything he’s done and I’m delighted he’ll continue presenting for the BBC.

“Amol has brought a real breath of fresh air to Today and we will keep evolving the programme as we continue to refresh the presenting team in the months ahead.”

Nick Robinson, Rajan’s Today colleague, wrote on X: “Missing you already @amolrajan – or should I say Del Boy?*

“You’ve added boundless energy, intelligence, curiosity, charm and quite a lot of bling to @BBCr4today

“Looking forward to watching your next adventure. Enjoy that sleep which you’ve been craving for so long. (*Watch the video!).”

During his time at Radio 4, Rajan presented several documentaries – The Decline of the West; Archive on 4 – 50 Years On: Rivers of Blood; and The Imperial Inversion of Cricket; as well as programmes Start the Week, PM, Any Answers, several series of Rethink and, for four years, The Media Show.

He presented the podcasts Harry, Meghan and the Media; and, with Robinson, The Today Podcast. For Radio 2, he has presented the Breakfast, Lunchtime and Drivetime shows.

In November, the presenter apologised live on air minutes after calling benefits claimants “scroungers”, saying it was “not the kind of language (he) would use”.

In the past, Rajan has criticised the BBC for being “too posh” and having an “accent bias”.

‘Anti-monarchist sentiments’

Mr Rajan himself has been criticised for his “anti-monarchist sentiments”.

In a 2012 column for the Independent examining the media’s relationship with the royals, he described the notion of a hereditary monarchy as “absurd”, the King as “scientifically illiterate” when he was the Prince of Wales, and Prince Philip a “racist buffoon”.

In other tweets and comment pieces, he called for the downfall of the “ridiculous House of Windsor”.

The presenter later apologised for the comments, describing them as “rude and immature”.

A decade later, Rajan wrote and presented The Princes and the Press, a two-part BBC series which Buckingham Palace, Kensington Palace and Clarence House described as “disappointing” for relying on “overblown and unfounded claims from unnamed sources”.

The documentary also dropped the term “Megxit” in favour of “Sussexit” to describe the pair’s departure from the Royal family.

Before joining the BBC, Rajan was appointed the youngest ever editor of a broadsheet title in Fleet Street when he took the reins at The Independent aged 29 in 2013.

Born in Calcutta and raised in Tooting, south London, Mr Rajan started out as a mic boy on Channel 5’s The Wright Stuff after graduating from Downing College, Cambridge.

Rajan is married to the academic Charlotte Faircloth, an associate professor at University College London’s Institute of Education, with whom he has four children.

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.

data.title: Hollywood billionaire’s battle royale over Warner Bros comes to Britain
data.description:
The billionaire boss of Paramount has held talks with the Culture Secretary to lobby against Netflix’s $83bn (£62bn) takeover of Warner Bros.

David Ellison, son of Oracle founder Larry Ellison, travelled to London on Thursday for a meeting with Lisa Nandy as part of a wider charm offensive designed to defeat Netflix in the escalating takeover battle.

Paramount is trying to convince shareholders to back its $30-per-share takeover offer and encourage regulators to obstruct Netflix’s rival bid, which was accepted by Warner Bros late last year.

Warner Bros has repeatedly rebuffed Paramount’s overtures, insisting that the $83bn deal agreed with Netflix last month remains superior.

Paramount’s campaign is centred on arguments that a merger between Netflix and Warner Bros would pose significant competition concerns owing to the combined company’s dominance in the streaming market.

Warner Bros owns Discovery+, which is home to TNT Sports, and HBO Max, a major US streaming service that is scheduled to launch in the UK in March.

Executives are also emphasising the impact of the deal on cinemas amid concerns that Netflix could reduce the number of films distributed for theatrical release.

In addition to the meeting with Ms Nandy, Paramount has met with officials at the Competition and Markets Authority and regulator Ofcom.

Senior Paramount figures also met with cinema executives who are fearful that Netflix will cut Warner Bros’ theatrical investments.

A source close to Paramount said the meetings were intended to build relationships after the Ellison family took control of the US studio in an $8bn deal last year.

Paramount owns Channel 5, while it has also made a number of major films in the UK, including the latest instalment in the Mission: Impossible franchise.

The planned takeover of Warner Bros will mark a major reshaping of Hollywood as traditional studios seek to build scale to compete in the cut-throat streaming era.

Any deal will also have significant implications in the UK, where Warner Bros employs around 4,000 people. The company has made a raft of blockbusters in its Leavesden studios near Watford in recent years, including Barbie and Wonka.

Paramount’s PR push

Netflix has played down competition concerns and insisted it is committed to releasing films in cinemas, though it has acknowledged that the regulatory review process could take up to 18 months.

Paramount would itself face regulatory scrutiny over its plans to merge two of Hollywood’s major studios, though insiders said they were confident the proposed deal would clear any reviews.

Mr Ellison and Paramount executives’ meetings form part of a broader charm offensive across Europe as the company seeks to shore up support from regulators in Brussels. Senior executives also recently met Emmanuel Macron, the French president, Bloomberg reported.

A source close to Paramount insisted Ms Nandy was the first port of call for Mr Ellison’s UK discussions, but did not rule out the possibility of a future meeting with Sir Keir Starmer.

The studio has tapped City PR firm Brunswick to help in its discussions, while US law firms Cravath, Swaine & Moore and Latham & Watkins are advising on the deal.

Paramount’s lead financial advisers are Centerview Partners and bankers at RedBird Capital, the US private equity firm led by Gerry Cardinale, which is also the company’s second-largest shareholder.

Mr Cardinale, who sits on Paramount’s board, is also thought to be working with Greenlight Group, a regulatory advisory launched late last year by Ali Nikpay, Daniel Gieve and Adam Atashzai.

Mr Gieve is a former chief executive of the Government’s Office for Investment.

Mr Atashzai also briefly held an advisory role in No10 under the Conservatives, while Mr Nikpay is a former partner at law firm Gibson Dunn.

Paramount ramped up its campaign this week by suing Warner Bros in an effort to secure “basic information” that it said had not been shared with investors.

It has also outlined plans to appoint directors to the Warner Bros board and fight a proxy battle against the Netflix deal.

However, the company was dealt a setback on Thursday when a Delaware judge rejected a request to fast-track its lawsuit. Paramount has given Warner Bros shareholders until January 21 to accept its bid, though this deadline could be extended.

Paramount and the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport declined to comment.

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: Soho House’s £1.3bn takeover revived after funding scramble
data.description:
A $1.8bn (£1.3bn) takeover of Soho House by the entrepreneur planning to transform the BT Tower has been revived following a funding scramble.

Soho House, which is listed on the New York Stock Exchange, has secured a new $200m funding commitment that will allow it to close a take-private deal with US hotel giant MCR Hotels.

Under the revised terms, Morse Ventures, an entity owned by MCR boss Tyler Morse, will commit $50m, while MCR will commit a further $50m.

Soho House has extended its debt facilities with private equity firm Apollo and Goldman Sachs from $150m to $220m, while Apollo will reduce its equity commitments.

Major shareholders including restaurateur Richard Caring have also agreed to roll over their stakes, reducing the amount of funding required for the deal by $50m.

The revised arrangements come a week after MCR, which owns the 1960s-themed TWA Hotel at JFK Airport and BT Tower in London, said it was unable to stump up the $200m it had promised for the deal.

The shock announcement threatened to scupper the long-awaited takeover of Soho House and sent shares tumbling. The company’s stock price bounced back after the new funding arrangement was confirmed.

The private member’s club, which was launched from a single venue on Greek Street in 1995, first announced in August that it would end its tumultuous time on the stock market after agreeing to be taken private by a consortium led by MCR.

The actor Ashton Kutcher is also investing in the deal while Soho House founder Nick Jones and billionaire executive chairman Ron Burkle are rolling over their stakes.

The $9-per-share deal marked an 83pc premium on the company’s share price. However, the sum was a far cry from the $13 reached when it floated in New York in July 2021.

The takeover is the latest deal struck by Mr Morse, a former ski instructor and baggage handler turned hotel tycoon.

MCR struck a £275m deal to buy the BT Tower in 2024, paving the way for the conversion of the Grade II-listed, 177-metre landmark into a hotel designed by architect Thomas Heatherwick.

Over three decades Soho House has grown into a sprawling empire with a network of 46 clubs around the world.

But its expansion came with growing pains as members complained that venues were becoming overcrowded and had lost their feeling of exclusivity.

Soho House responded by temporarily halting applications for memberships at its clubs in London, New York and Los Angeles.

It has since started accepting new members again and has opened new venues including Soho Mews House and Soho Farmhouse Ibiza.
